facts: Rivian Automotive, Inc. is an American electric vehicle manufacturer and automotive technology company founded in 2009.
It produces an electric sport utility vehicle (SUV) and a pickup truck on a skateboard platform that can support future vehicles and be adopted by other companies.
The company produces an electric delivery van, the Rivian EDV, which is sold to Amazon for deliveries.
Rivian started deliveries of its R1T pickup truck in late 2021.
The company planned to build an exclusive charging network in the United States and Canada by the end of 2023.

Rivian is based in Irvine, California, with its manufacturing plant in Normal, Illinois, and other facilities in Palo Alto, California; Carson, California; Plymouth, Michigan; Burnaby, British Columbia; Wittmann, Arizona; Woking, England; and Belgrade, Serbia.
Rivian has plans to build another US$5 billion factory in Social Circle, Georgia.
The company raised over US$13.5 billion in financing following its initial public offering in November 2021.
The company was founded in Rockledge, Florida in 2009 as Mainstream Motors by RJ Scaringe.
After being renamed Avera Automotive or Avera Motors, the company became Rivian Automotive in 2011.
Rivian's first car model was intended to be a sports car dubbed the R1, which was shelved in late 2011.
In 2015, Rivian received a large investment and opened research facilities in Michigan and the San Francisco Bay Area.
Shortly after 2015, Rivian began working exclusively on electric autonomous vehicles.
By September 2016, Rivian was negotiating to buy a manufacturing plant formerly owned by Mitsubishi Motors in Normal, Illinois.
In January 2017, Rivian acquired the Normal plant and its machinery for $16 million to become its primary North American manufacturing facility.
In December 2017, Rivian revealed its first two products: an electric five-passenger pickup truck and an electric seven-passenger SUV, provisionally named A1T and A1C.
In November 2018, the truck and SUV were renamed R1T and R1S and unveiled at the LA Auto Show.
Production for the R1T and R1S was scheduled to begin in 2020.
Rivian had 250 employees at the start of 2018, growing to 750 by February 2019, over 3,000 by November 2020, and over 9,000 by November 2021.
In late 2020, Rivian planned to begin R1T shipments in June 2021, which slipped to August and was delayed again due to a global microchip shortage.
In September 2021, Rivian became the first automaker to bring a fully electric pickup to the consumer market.
In October 2021, Rivian began delivering the R1T truck to customers.
On November 10, 2021, Rivian went public via an IPO, selling 153 million shares at $78.00 and valuing the company at $66.5 billion.
Rivian's stock began trading on Nasdaq under the ticker RIVN and closed its first day at $100.73 per share, valuing the company at just under $100 billion.
On November 19, 2021, Ford and Rivian announced they no longer planned to co-develop an electric vehicle, with Ford retaining a 12 percent stake valued at over $10 billion.
Ford sold around 90 percent of its Rivian stake for $3 billion by the end of 2022, up from its initial $1.2 billion investment.
In December 2021, as Rivian began delivering R1S SUVs and ramping production, COO Rod Copes stepped down and retired.
On March 1, 2022, Rivian announced price increases of 17 percent for the R1T and 20 percent for the R1S due to semiconductor shortages and higher component costs.
Rivian later apologized for the retroactive price increases and honored original configured prices for existing preorders.
On March 14, 2022, Rivian hired Frank Klein as COO, who began on June 1, 2022.
In March 2022, Rivian was named to TIME's List of 100 Most Influential Companies.
On July 27, 2022, Rivian announced a 6 percent workforce reduction in response to high inflation, rising interest rates, and increased parts prices.
In September 2022, Rivian signed an MoU with Mercedes-Benz Group for a joint venture to invest in and operate a European factory for large commercial electric vans.
Rivian cancelled the Mercedes-Benz partnership in December 2022 but stated future collaborations may be possible.
In October 2022, Rivian issued a voluntary recall of 13,000 vehicles due to seven reports of loose torque bolts.
In January 2023, reports indicated the departure of multiple top executives during a challenging period for the company.
On June 20, 2023, Rivian announced it would incorporate Tesla's NACS charging standard into R1T and R1S vehicles in 2025 and its upcoming R2 platform.
As early as Q2 2024, Rivian owners may be able to use adapters to connect to Tesla's Supercharger network.
On June 21, 2023, Rivian announced the acquisition of Swedish mapping company Iternio and its ABRP app, integrating it into its in-car navigation system.
In late 2023, Amazon's total EDV fleet size reached 10,000 units.
In October 2023, Rivian ended its exclusivity deal with Amazon, allowing it to supply other commercial customers with electric delivery vehicles.
In December 2023, AT&T announced it would take delivery of Rivian trucks for a fleet trial process.
In October 2023, Rivian announced plans to open a new Georgia manufacturing facility with an annual production capacity of 400,000 units in early 2024.
In March 2026, plans for vertical construction to begin by the end of 2026 were announced for the Georgia facility at a Joint Development Authority meeting.
On February 21, 2024, Rivian announced it would lay off 10 percent of its salaried workers starting February 23, 2024.
In March 2024, the Georgia factory project was put on hold for financial reasons but restarted in November 2024 following a $6 billion federal loan announcement.
On March 7, 2024, Rivian unveiled the R2 SUV starting from $45,000 and announced an even less expensive R3 model.
On June 25, 2024, Volkswagen Group announced an intention to invest up to $5 billion in Rivian, with an initial $1 billion and additional $4 billion anticipated by 2026.
The Volkswagen investment includes a prospective joint venture aimed at developing electrical architecture and software technology.
In July 2024, Germany's competition authority gave approval for the form of the Rivian-Volkswagen joint venture.
In February 2025, Rivian opened orders for its Electric Commercial Van (ECV) to business fleet customers.

In July 2025, Rivian announced plans to establish an East Coast headquarters at the Junction Krog District in Atlanta, Georgia, with a 45,000 ft2 office opening in late 2025 and plans to accommodate 500 employees by the end of 2026.
For Q1 2026, Rivian produced 10,236 vehicles and delivered 10,365, reaffirming annual delivery guidance of between 62,000 and 67,000 vehicles for the 2026 fiscal year.
In December 2025, Rivian announced its first Autonomy and AI Day to showcase autonomous capabilities with an end-to-end AI-centric approach.
On December 9, 2025, CEO RJ Scaringe announced his goal of creating an autonomous vehicle capable of running and coordinating tasks and errands for people.
Both R1 models are designed to be capable off-road with 14 inches of ground clearance.
The R1T truck was claimed to accelerate from 0-60 mph in under 3 seconds, wade through 3 ft 7 in of water, and climb a 45-degree incline.
The R1T has a motor at each of its four wheels, with each wheel controllable independently.
Most expensive R1 models reach around 450 miles on a charge and feature an 800HP electric motor.
Rivian designed its vehicles to facilitate carsharing with autonomous features.
The Rivian EDV is designed in three sizes capable of carrying 500, 700, or 900 cubic feet of packages.
All three EDV sizes share their basic electrical and network architecture, ECUs, and battery packs with the R1 models.
In December 2021, Rivian reported over 71,000 pre-orders for its R1 models.
The company produced a total of 24,337 cars in 2022.
The R1T features four electric motors producing a combined 835 hp and 908 lb-ft of torque, with a claimed 0-60 mph time of 3.0 seconds.
The R1T is offered with three battery sizes: 105 kWh, 135 kWh, or 180 kWh, with projected ranges of 230, 314, and over 400 miles respectively.
In December 2021, the R1T was named Motor Trend Truck of the Year.
In August 2022, Rivian eliminated the base Explore package of the R1T due to low customer demand to streamline supply chain issues.
The R1S aims for 91 percent shared components with the R1T, sharing a chassis that includes braking, suspension, and cooling systems with a central battery.
In February 2019, Rivian entered a commercial agreement with Amazon to design, produce, and purchase 100,000 electric delivery vehicles.
As of 2019, Amazon expected up to 10,000 electric vans in operation by 2022, with full delivery of the 100,000 contract by 2030.
In November 2022, Rivian announced it had delivered 1,000 EDVs to Amazon.
In 2026, Amazon announced it reached half its goal with over 50,000 EV delivery vans in its fleet as part of a plan to convert its delivery fleet to 100 percent renewable energy by 2030.
The EDV assembly line is designed as a low-feature-content line to keep costs down and allow Amazon to reduce costs and shrink its carbon footprint.
A 150-mile prototype for the Amazon EDV was tested on public roads in early 2021 across multiple U.S. cities including Los Angeles, San Francisco, Colorado, Oklahoma, and Michigan.
Deployment of electric delivery vans began in nine U.S. cities in July 2022.
Amazon also sources electric vans from other OEMs such as Stellantis, Mercedes-Benz Group, and Mahindra.
In early 2023, Amazon notified Rivian it wanted to buy about 10,000 vans that year, prompting Rivian to seek removal of exclusivity terms.
By January 2024, Amazon agreed to end the exclusivity arrangement, allowing Rivian to seek new commercial customers.
The R2 is a smaller, less-expensive SUV on a new platform launched in mid-year 2026 with initial production at the Normal, IL facility.
In May 2023, a shrouded clay model of the R2 was shown, with expected launch costs between $40,000 and $60,000 and a potential pickup truck version alongside the SUV.

In March 2021, Rivian announced plans for a public charging network across the U.S. and Canada by the end of 2023, initially restricted to owners but open to any EV with a CCS port or adapter as of 2026.
The charging network targets 600 Adventure Network sites with 3,500 DC fast chargers at high-traffic locations, starting at 200 kW with a target of 300 kW.
Rivian planned 10,000 destination chargers called Rivian Waypoints at retail, lodging, dining, and parks, usable by all EVs with a J1772 connector.
In July 2021, Rivian announced plans to install Waypoint chargers at all 56 Tennessee state parks, with the first site unveiled on October 1, 2021.
In June 2023, it was reported that Rivian adopted NACS for its vehicles in North America beginning in the 2026 model year.
As of 2026, Rivian had more than 50,000 CCS DC fast chargers across the United States, all powered by renewable energy.
Rivian's headquarters in Irvine, California focuses on vehicle engineering and design, propulsion and battery system development, and commercial functions.

The sole production factory in Normal, Illinois manufactures components like battery packs in a 2.6-million-square-foot plant equipped with paint shops, robotics, stamping machines, and injection molding equipment.
In July 2020, plans for 576,000 ft2 of additions to the Normal facility were approved by the town council.
In October 2021, Rivian announced plans for a 623,000 ft2 addition to the Normal facility, bringing total size to 3,900,000 ft2.
In December 2021, Rivian announced plans for a $5 billion battery and assembly plant east of Atlanta with construction planned for summer 2022 and production starting in 2024.
The Georgia plant was projected to employ 7,500 workers with possible growth to 10,000 workers.
Some locals opposed the Rivian plant due to environmental concerns and government costs, including Georgia proposing to allocate $125 million for associated factory costs.
In early 2024, Rivian announced it was putting construction of the Georgia plant on hold, noting the land had been at least 90 percent graded, while all vehicles continued to be made in Normal.
Cumulative vehicle production reached 132,048 by Q4 2024 and 152,638 by Q2 2025.
As of December 2021, Rivian is a public company trading under the symbol RIVN on Nasdaq.
From seed stage to its final $2.5 billion round in 2021, Rivian secured 10 rounds of private funding.
In January 2017, Rivian received a $1 million grant and five-year tax abatement from Normal, Illinois contingent on employment targets and a $40.5 million investment over five years.
Rivian also received $49.5 million in tax credits from the Illinois state government contingent on meeting employment targets and investing at least $175 million into the site by 2024.
In December 2017, Sumitomo Corporation made a large strategic investment in Rivian, though the amount was undisclosed.
On May 23, 2018, Rivian closed on $200 million in debt financing from Standard Chartered Bank, bringing total raised funds to upwards of $450 million.
On February 15, 2019, Amazon led an investment round of $700 million into Rivian.
On April 24, 2019, Ford Motor Company invested $500 million at a pre-money valuation of $4.5 billion.
On September 10, 2019, Cox Automotive invested $350 million into Rivian and planned to add a representative to its board.
In December 2019, Rivian raised US$1.3 billion in a round led by T Rowe Price with Ford and Amazon.

On January 19, 2021, Rivian raised $2.65 billion led by T. Rowe Price alongside multiple institutional investors including Soros Fund Management, Fidelity, Coatue Management, BlackRock, Abdul Latif Jameel, Amazon Climate Pledge Fund, and D1 Capital Partners.

On July 23, 2021, Rivian raised $2.5 billion in a round led by Amazon's Climate Pledge Fund, D1 Capital Partners, and Ford, with new investors Third Point Ventures and Dragoneer Investment Group joining.
In August 2021, Rivian filed for an IPO seeking a valuation as high as $80 billion after raising approximately $11 billion total.
Amazon purchased $200 million of stock in the IPO, raising its total stake in Rivian to 22 percent.
Rivian's stock peaked at $146.07 on November 13, 2021, before falling continuously during the early 2022 tech sell-off to $30.68 on April 26, 2022.
The market value declined to roughly $29 billion by the end of April 2022, a loss of 79 percent from its peak.
At the beginning of May 2022, Rivian's stock price fell further to an all-time low of $22.78 on May 9, 2022, a decline of nearly 85 percent from the November 2021 peak.
Rivian's stock recovered significantly through the first half of 2023, reaching a period high of $28.06 on July 27, 2023.
Momentum reversed with a poorly received $1.5 billion convertible green note, causing the share price to decline to a daily low of $15.88 on October 26, 2023.
Around 28 percent of Rivian shares are held by insiders, with the 10 largest institutional shareholders holding significant portions.

facts: Stryten Energy is the only company with a complete offering of advanced lead, lithium and vanadium batteries.

Stryten Energy’s Vanadium Redox Flow Battery (VRFB) is designed for medium- to long-duration energy storage from 4 to 12 hours for applications including microgrids, utility-scale storage, data centers and military bases.
Stryten Energy offers innovative lithium-ion batteries for military and large-scale operations requiring 24/7 performance, material handling and transportation applications.

Stryten Energy’s advanced lead battery portfolio includes Enhanced Flooded Batteries (EFB) for start-stop applications, tubular technology for material handling and Valve Regulated AGM for backup power in microgrids, utilities and data centers.
Stryten Energy’s inCOMMAND software leverages real fleet data to optimize motive power batteries and chargers through a central dashboard for performance monitoring and management.

Stryten Energy announced plans on March 31, 2025, to add 10 gigawatts of new U.S. energy storage manufacturing capacity to support military, long-duration energy storage, transportation and material-handling applications.

Stryten Energy launched Storion Energy in a joint venture with Largo on December 19, 2024, to establish a domestic supply of vanadium electrolyte for long-duration energy storage via a unique leasing model.

Stryten Energy was awarded DOE MAKE IT Prize Phase 2 funding on October 2, 2024, for its Securing America’s Vanadium Electrolyte Supply (SAVES) project to scale U.S.-based production of vanadium redox flow battery electrolyte.

Stryten Energy secured exclusive rights to market and distribute Battle Born Batteries® for military, automotive, marine, power sports, lawn & garden and golf cart applications through a licensing agreement with Dragonfly Energy.
Stryten Energy invested in REVKIT on October 15, 2024, to become the preferred provider of advanced automotive batteries on the platform's digital marketplace.
Stryten Energy’s premium lead battery and charger solutions will be distributed through KION North America’s network of 200 sales and service locations nationwide.
Stryten Energy’s M-Series Li600 lithium-ion material handling batteries were certified for fleet use and made available through Hyster-Yale as of June 17, 2025.
Stryten Energy launched its new M-Series Li610 lithium-ion battery on April 13, 2026, expanding its motive power portfolio for material handling operations with longer cycle life.
Stryten Energy introduced the E-Series AGM160/190 multi-terminal batteries designed for harsh environments and long-duration discharge applications in telecommunications and utility sectors.
Stryten Energy installed a lead Battery Energy Storage System (BESS) at the VICARS Community Center in Southwest Atlanta to provide power resiliency for the community.

Georgia Institute of Technology and Stryten Energy announced the successful installation of a lead battery energy storage system on April 23, 2025, to support advanced research and carbon-neutral goals alongside existing solar infrastructure.
Stryten Energy hosted U.S. Senator Charles Grassley at its transportation manufacturing plant in Manchester, Iowa, which recently celebrated 50 years of battery production.
The Kansas House of Representatives issued House Resolution No. 6036 on March 30, 2026, honoring Stryten Energy’s 50 years of manufacturing excellence at its Salina, Kansas plant.
Stryten Energy welcomed 25 summer interns in 2025, with 18 assigned to work at its corporate headquarters in Alpharetta, GA.

Stryten Energy opened an expanded, state-of-the-art battery testing lab at its new corporate headquarters in Alpharetta, Ga., featuring dedicated space for testing lead, lithium and vanadium batteries and materials.

Stryten Energy’s mobile microgrid solution is designed for remote power and disaster relief scenarios, demonstrating a hybrid diesel-electric vehicle ecosystem that manages energy sources, storage and usage.

The U.S. Department of Energy includes lead batteries among critical technologies for domestic supply chains and national security, highlighting the importance of North America’s closed-loop lead-battery recycling system.
Stryten Energy highlighted scalable, technology-agnostic BESS solutions supporting data centers, manufacturing and EV charging at CES 2026.
U.S. power demand is projected to reach 4,185 billion kilowatt-hours (kWh) in 2025, a record high compared to 4,067 billion kWh in 2022, driving demand for energy storage solutions.
China’s export ban on critical minerals including antimony, gallium and germanium, effective December 3, 2024, is disrupting global battery manufacturing supply chains.
Stryten Energy showcased advancements to its M-Series X-7 charger at MODEX 2026, highlighting updates that expand compatibility, power and flexibility for material handling applications.
Stryten Energy introduced a new Material Handling Battery Finder tool at MODEX 2026 (Booth #B9325) to help users quickly match batteries to trucks.
Enhanced Flooded Batteries (EFB) offer a lower-cost alternative to AGM with about 50% higher capacity and up to 52% longer cycle life for start-stop and hybrid vehicles.
Stryten Energy’s inCOMMAND™ platform received major performance and usability enhancements on August 25, 2025, enabling more efficient fleet management for motive power batteries and chargers.
Stryten Energy delivered battery-first technologies for commercial, industrial and utility-scale applications to BCI Flow Batteries North America as of October 27, 2025.
The Inflation Reduction Act provides incentives like the Advanced Manufacturing Production Credit (AMPC) for domestic clean energy manufacturing investments.

Syensqo is a Belgian multinational chemicals and advanced materials company founded in December 2023 through a spin-off from Solvay.
The company's CEO is Mike Radossich, who succeeded Ilham Kadri in January 2026, with Heike Van de Kerkhof serving as Chair of the Board.
Syensqo went public on December 11, 2023, listing on Euronext Brussels and joining the BEL20 index before voluntarily delisting from Euronext Paris on September 16, 2024.
The company operates globally with 54 industrial sites, 12 major R&D centers, and 13,000 employees across 30 countries, reporting €5,969 million in revenue for 2025.

Syensqo's business focuses on specialty polymers, composites, and specialty chemicals, with growth platforms in batteries, green hydrogen, thermoplastic composites, renewable materials, and biotechnology.

The company produces high-performance composite materials including thermoset and thermoplastic composites, carbon fiber prepregs, resin systems, and adhesives for automotive, aerospace, and energy applications.

Syensqo manufactures specialty polymers such as fluoropolymers (PVDF, ECTFE, PTFE), fluoroelastomers (FKM), perfluoroelastomers (FFKM), and fluorinated fluids (PFPE) used in electronics, energy, chemical processing, and semiconductor manufacturing.
The company introduced fluorinated elastomers manufactured without certain fluorosurfactants associated with PFAS chemistry.
Syensqo produces perfluorosulfonic acid (PFSA) ionomers used as ion-conducting materials in electrochemical technologies like fuel cells and water electrolysis.
Electroactive polymers (EAPs) produced by the company are utilized in sensors, actuators, and related devices.
Syensqo supplies specialty chemical reagents used in the recovery and separation of critical battery metals including lithium, nickel, and cobalt.
The company develops bio-based and circular materials incorporating renewable or recycled feedstocks for applications in automotive, transportation, and industrial sectors.
In the United States, Syensqo operates 26 production sites and 9 R&D centers across locations including California, Minnesota, Ohio, Tennessee, and Georgia.
The company maintains facilities in Alpharetta and Winder, Georgia, alongside a dedicated site in Augusta, Georgia that supports materials used in electric-vehicle battery applications.
Syensqo became a sponsor of the DS Penske Formula E racing team in January 2024 through a three-year partnership agreement.
The company's bio-based carbon fiber composites have been utilized in Mercedes-AMG's W16 race car.

In May 2024, Syensqo served as the main partner alongside Mohammed VI Polytechnic University and OCP Group for the Climate Impulse project, aiming to fly a green hydrogen-powered aircraft around the world by 2028.
The company established a Scientific Advisory Board in June 2024 featuring academics Ben Feringa, Avelino Corma, Karim Zaghib, and Juan de Pablo to support innovation projects.
Syensqo manufactures components for the Hermes 450 drone at its UK facility for UAV Tactical Systems, a company co-owned by Elbits Systems Ltd and Thales.

The company recognizes scientific advancements through the Science for the Future Ernest Solvay Prize, with recent recipients including Xiaowei Zhuang (2026), Omar Yaghi (2024), Katalin Karikó (2022), and others.
Headquartered in Alpharetta, GA.
Reported earnings results for the three months ended March 31, 2024.
Completed the sale of its Engineered Papers business on November 30, 2023.
Q1 2024 sales were $500.2 million, increasing 10.6% sequentially versus Q4 2023 and decreasing 8.9% versus the prior year.
GAAP loss was $(28.0) million with a GAAP EPS of $(0.52), including organizational realignment, integration and purchase accounting expenses.
Adjusted income was $1.1 million, Adjusted EPS was $0.02, and Adjusted EBITDA was $45.8 million.
Adjusted EBITDA was down 6% versus the prior year as impacts from lower volumes and higher production costs more than offset net benefits of input costs/selling price and synergies realization.
CEO Julie Schertell commented that meaningful sequential volume improvement occurred in Q1 and expects to deliver meaningfully improved EBITDA year-over-year for the remainder of 2024.
Initiated an organizational restructuring initiative expected to reduce non-operating costs by up to $20 million annually as they exit 2024.
Actions taken in 2023 included divesting Engineered Papers, consolidating footprint, and investing in new assets in filtration and release liners.
Filtration & Advanced Materials (FAM) segment sales were $202.7 million, up 11% sequentially versus Q4 2023 and down 8% versus the prior year period.
FAM products include filtration media and components, advanced films, coating and converting solutions, and extruded mesh products.
GAAP Operating Profit in FAM included $3.2 million of restructuring, impairment, and other expenses primarily related to organizational realignment and footprint rationalization.
Sustainable & Adhesive Solutions (SAS) segment sales were $297.5 million, up 10% sequentially versus Q4 2023 and down 9% versus the prior year period.
SAS products include tapes, labels, liners, specialty paper, packaging and healthcare solutions.
GAAP Operating Profit in SAS included $7.6 million in restructuring, impairment, and other expenses primarily related to organizational realignment and footprint rationalization.

Adjusted unallocated expenses were in line with the prior year, while GAAP operating expenses included $1.5 million of EP divestiture expenses, $3.1 million in restructuring/impairment expenses, and $4.4 million related to organizational realignment.
Interest expense was $18.3 million versus $15.6 million in the prior year period, primarily due to higher average interest rates and higher average balances on floating debt.
Tax rate was 7.9% during Q1 2024, driven by one-time tax adjustments; excluding these, the rate was 16.8%.
Year-to-date 2024 cash used in operating activities was $13.0 million, with capital spending and software costs totaling $12.6 million.
Total debt was $1,158.0 million as of March 31, 2024, with total cash of $128.9 million resulting in net debt of $1,029.1 million.
Total liquidity was approximately $409 million, consisting of $129 million of cash and $280 million of revolver availability.
Company's debt matures on a staggered basis between 2026 and 2028.
Announced next quarterly cash dividend of $0.10 per share payable on June 21, 2024 to stockholders of record as of May 24, 2024.
Did not repurchase shares during the first quarter but intends to periodically repurchase shares to offset dilution due to stock compensation.
Will hold a conference call to review Q1 2024 results at 8:30 a.m. Eastern time on Thursday, May 9, 2024.

canonical_name: DOE OCED eXCHANGE Teaming Partners Directory
facts: ABB Inc is a large business focused on carbon capture, storage, and transport, with over 105,000 employees globally.

ABB offers smart sensing devices and solutions across chemical, oil & gas, water, power generation, cement, pulp & paper, marine, hydrogen, and battery industries through its Measurement & Analytical division.
ABB's US footprint includes a manufacturing facility and R&D site in Bartlesville, OK, and a Corporate Research team in Raleigh, NC.
ABB is interested in partnering on point source carbon capture technology and CO2 transport and storage (Topics 1, 2 & 3 of DE-FOA-0003473).
Imperial College London operates a pilot plant equipped with over 250 ABB instruments and sensors to advance carbon capture technology research.

The Emissions Capture Company Group, LTD (ECCO) is a UK-based large business commercializing technologies that capture industrial emissions and waste into valuable chemicals, with US operations in Texas and South Carolina.
ECCO is interested in developing technologies at laboratory, pilot, and full scales based on economic viability from selling chemicals and biomass.

ECCO's current focus areas include waste-to-products from natural gas refining (CO2 conversion, H2S pyrolysis, Methane pyrolysis), ammonia synthesis from air and water, brewery waste to organic fertilizer conversion, and AI/ML in technology development.
ECCO has capabilities for process architecture design through detailed engineering and project execution.
ECCO's current scaled project in Texas captures waste CO2 and converts it into baking soda, with numerous proposed sites in rural Energy Communities.
Energy Systems Network is a non-profit focused on grid industrial energy efficiency via smart manufacturing tools.
Its Energy Insights program helps manufacturers understand energy consumption using IIOT sensors and standard smart manufacturing SCADA systems.

The State of Indiana provides funding for the program, which includes an Energy Monitoring and Smart Manufacturing Starter Kit, a base project for data visualization and analysis, system integrator services, and a 4-part, 12-hour training course.

Texas A&M University Professor Sheng-Jen Hsieh directs the Rockwell Automation Laboratory and researches industrial control, cyber-physical systems, machine learning, Industry 4.0, optical/infrared imaging, and automation/robotics education.

Dr. Hsieh has been awarded over $6.0M in research funding, resulting in over 200 publications, and serves as an Associate Editor for Industrial Robot and Editorial Board member for Assembly Automation and Sensor Review.

University of Minnesota Research Program Manager Matthew Aro's team has expertise in manufacturing carbon-sequestering biochar at lab- and pilot-scales and analyzing it for water treatment, concrete/building materials, steel production reducing agent, and pollution remediation.

The University of Minnesota team modifies biochar to impart new properties, analyzes performance of new materials incorporating biochar, and conducts community demonstrations of new biochar-containing materials.
Southern Methodist University Professor Saeed Salehi holds the Herman Brown Endowed Chair of Engineering and researches carbon capture, utilization, and storage (CCUS) related to cement and concrete.

Dr. Salehi's CCUS expertise includes CO2 capture technologies, carbonation in concrete, sustainable cement alternatives, CO2 mineralization, life cycle assessment, policy/regulation, and pilot/commercial scale implementation.
Dr. Salehi has served as PI/Co-PI on R&D projects totaling over $10 million funded by the DOE, Department of the Interior, and energy industry companies.
World Resources Institute Senior Manager Dr. Ankita Gangotra's team conducts analysis and policy development to accelerate decarbonization in chemicals, refining, cement, and steel sectors.

WRI published first-of-a-kind analysis on using Low Carbon Fuels, Feedstocks and Energy Sources (LCFFES) to defossilize chemicals and evaluated clean hydrogen capacity needs for fuel/feedstock switching.
WRI's current work focuses on developing data disclosure and demand-side policy recommendations to incentivize low-emission chemicals development.

WRI co-convened the Industrial Innovation Initiative (i3) over four years, convening industrial companies, NGOs, and labor organizations to develop consensus-based industrial decarbonization policies.

WRI participates in the World Economic Forum industrial clusters project and collaborates on hydrogen demand convenings, alongside involvement with the Bipartisan Policy Center and American Petroleum Institute roundtables.
Rocky Mountain Institute (RMI) is a nonprofit founded in 1982 focused on catalyzing market-based change to align critical geographies with a 1.5°C future.
RMI works to cut greenhouse gas emissions at least 50% by 2030 through decarbonizing energy production and use across power, transportation, buildings, and industry sectors.

RMI's Cement Initiative supports sector decarbonization via research, policy measures, industry coalitions, and public commitments to the 1.5°C target, with past work including technoeconomic analysis for CEMEX.
The RMI team has prior experience on federally and philanthropically funded projects amounting to over $560M, covering business cases, community benefits, and technical components.
RMI's expertise includes electricity generation, hydrogen, transportation, steel, market adoption, SCMs, alternative heat sources, CCUS, and alternative binders.
Potential collaboration areas with RMI include technoeconomic analysis, facilitating demand aggregation, developing supply chains, and supporting policy design.
Industrial Microbes is a small business building new microbes for sustainable chemical production to accelerate decarbonization in the chemical industry.
The company specializes in fermentation and downstream processes that compete with petrochemicals on price, focusing on decarbonizing building-block chemicals like 3HP for acrylic acid production.
Industrial Microbes partners with chemical producers to co-develop and scale bio-manufacturing processes, leveraging microbial engineering expertise to bring sustainable products to market.

Metals Innovation Initiative (MI2) is a Kentucky-based nonprofit providing collaborative, industry-led executive leadership to attract advanced research, sustainability, commercialization, and talent development in Kentucky's metals industry.
Kentucky's metals industry includes more than 250 facilities employing over 36,000 people statewide.

MI2 was formed in 2022 by eight Kentucky metals companies (Kobe Aluminum Automotive Products, Logan Aluminum, North American Stainless, Novelis, Nucor, River Metals Recycling, Tri-Arrows Aluminum, and Wieland) with support from the Kentucky Governor and Cabinet for Economic Development.
MI2 is administered through the Kentucky Science and Technology Corporation and originated from a whitepaper by AccelerateKY based on MIT Regional Entrepreneurship Acceleration Program principles.

Stevens Institute of Technology Associate Professor Weina Meng holds an NSF CAREER award and researches sustainable construction materials using industrial by-products like slag, fly ash, waste concrete, and biomass in cement/concrete applications.

Dr. Meng's work aims to mitigate environmental impact while enhancing material performance through CO2 sequestration, waste utilization, rapid surface modification technologies for solid wastes, and multiscale experimentation with computational modeling and machine learning.
PHNX Materials, INC is a small business refining 2.5B tons of US landfilled coal ash into valuable end-products like ASTM-grade ash, titanium, aluminum, and rare-earth elements.
PHNX Materials completed a pre-seed round raising $2.45M to produce high-quality supplementary cementitious materials (SCMs) from landfilled coal fly-ash.
The company has developed technology to strip major impurities from landfilled CFA and formed partnerships with 5 utilities providing landfilled CFA for processing.
PHNX Materials is interested in decarbonizing cement and producing high-quality metal feedstocks.

University of Miami Assistant Professor Sivakumar Ramanathan specializes in low carbon concrete materials, including SCMs, SCM reactivity, recycled concrete aggregate, CO2 sequestration in concrete, durability, cellulose nanomaterials, and biochar.

Auburn University Professor Jeyhoon's group studies transport phenomena in energy-intensive processing industries (metals, cement/concrete, chemicals), including mathematical/physical modeling of tundish liquid aluminum flows, continuous steel casters, thermophysical properties, and latent heat thermal energy storage systems from 250-1500 oF.
Dr. Jeyhoon's grant sponsors include the Alabama Space Grant Consortium, ALCOA, California Energy Commission, DOE NREL, NSF, ONR, NASA, and US Forest Service.
Ozinga is a 5th generation American family-owned construction material business founded in 1928, operating over 1000 mixer trucks from 100 plants across the Midwest (IL, IN, WI, MI) and Florida.
Ozinga is a leader in developing low-carbon construction materials, including converting transport fuel to renewable natural gas and growing alternative SCMs to reduce carbon impact.

ONB Engineering offers techno-economic analysis, lifecycle assessment, and market readiness validation for carbon management and industrial decarbonization technologies using advanced custom modeling tools.

ONB Engineering specializes in technical feasibility assessment, reactor/process level modeling, R&D assessment, comprehensive techno-economic/LCA assessments, and engineering package planning (FEL 1-3).
The company utilizes commercial computational tools like Aspen Plus, DWSIM, Barracuda, and Matlab, and its engineers hold PE, PMP, and Six Sigma certifications.

University of Miami Associate Professor Prannoy Suraneni is a worldwide expert on concrete decarbonization, SCMs, and cements, with expertise in mechanochemical activation, thermal activation, and CO2 uptake for novel cement manufacturing.
Dr. Suraneni's lab has tested over 1000 different materials for SCM feasibility and developed numerous test methods to rapidly measure SCM reactivity linked to strength and durability.

The University of Miami Civil and Architectural Engineering Department has five other faculty members working on concrete decarbonization, with expertise spanning molecular dynamics to large-scale testing and life-cycle analysis.
AggrePlex LLC produces PozzoDyne™, an Activated Ground Glass Pozzolan made from diverted landfill waste glass that serves as an OPC alternative and mitigates Alkali-Silica Reaction.
PozzoDyne™ is derived from finely ground waste glass (<20 microns), reduces carbon emissions by 92% per ton compared to traditional cement, and lowers concrete global warming potential by up to 20%.

AggrePlex plans to establish 100,000-ton/year AGGP facilities in Florida, California, Georgia, Texas, Pennsylvania, and Illinois, processing post-consumer container glass and expanding to windshield, solar panel, and fiberglass glass.

AggrePlex leadership includes CEO Kevin Crutchfield, President Anthony Cialone, EVP James Burnham, CTO Josef Fischer, Director of Business Development Herb Northrop, Director of Plant Operations David Petroni, and Director of LEAN Manufacturing & Quality Production Koren Wah.
The company aims to increase glass recovery rates from 25% to 75%, create high-paying technical jobs, and support sustainability throughout the United States.
Lakril Technologies Corporation is a small business focused on industrial decarbonization through petrochemical disruption using bio-based sugars as the backbone for high-volume chemicals.

entity_name: Noise-Damping Interior Insulation and Panel Systems Market
canonical_name: Noise-Damping Interior Insulation and Panel Systems Market
facts: The Noise-Damping Interior Insulation and Panel Systems Market stood at USD 4.7 billion in 2025.
The market value is expected to reach USD 5.0 billion in 2026.
The market value is expected to reach USD 8.8 billion by 2036.
The market is expanding at a 5.8% CAGR from 2026 to 2036.
Growth is driven by rising cabin quietness needs and stronger use of lightweight acoustic materials.
OEM line fit accounts for an 83.7% share of the market.
Passenger cars contribute a 62.4% share of the market.
Automakers are focusing on comfort and lower cabin vibration.
Cabin noise control is becoming more difficult as vehicle makers reduce weight and use more recyclable materials.
Recyclable acoustic systems are gaining interest because they help reduce cabin noise and support end-of-life material recovery.
Autoneum reported in 2025 that its Propylat PET interior solution uses 100% polyester and absorbs tire and electric motor noise.
This gives OEMs a practical way to combine sound control with recycling goals.
Interior air quality is affecting material choice.
BASF stated in 2025 that its Elastoflex® E acoustic foam system achieves fogging values below 0.5 mg under DIN 75201.
This helps vehicle makers manage cabin comfort and interior emission needs through one material platform.
Suppliers with recyclable designs and tested low-emission acoustic materials are likely to gain stronger access to future vehicle interior programs.
Noise-damping interior insulation and panel systems cover acoustic absorbers and trim-backed panels installed inside vehicles.
Parts reduce road noise and airflow sound inside the cabin.
Scope covers floor mats and dash insulators.
Door pads and headliner absorbers are included.
Trunk liners and wheelhouse interior systems are included as engineered vehicle parts.
Market inclusions cover noise-damping interior insulation and panel systems categorized by system type.
Floor systems and dash panels are included.
Door pads, headliners, and trunk liners complete the system scope.
Material type covers foam fiber composites and polyester felt.
Rubber mats, natural fiber, and recycled PET complete the material scope.
Vehicle class covers passenger cars and SUVs.
Light trucks, commercial vehicles, and electric vehicles complete the vehicle scope.
Application area includes floor and dash, doors and roof, trunk area, wheelhouse liners, and cockpit panels.
The scope includes cabin insulation parts integrated within automotive interior trim parts.
Sound control and vibration reduction work inside one comfort system.
Revenue scope covers 2026 to 2036.
Key stakeholders include automakers, tier 1 interior suppliers, acoustic material converters, trim module makers, material suppliers, vehicle design teams, and cabin NVH testing providers.
Scope excludes exterior sound barriers and industrial plant silencers unless sold into vehicle interiors.
General acoustic wall coverings are excluded.
Mechanical seat structures and electronic noise cancellation are excluded unless bundled with an interior acoustic panel package.
Cabin damping demand is rising as electric vehicles change what drivers hear inside vehicles.
IEA reported a 65.0% battery electric share of electric car sales in 2025.
Engine noise drops sharply in battery models so tire roar and panel vibration become more obvious.
Acoustic packages need earlier placement in platform design.
Suppliers with interior NVH testing systems can prove performance before tooling release.
Global vehicle output gives acoustic suppliers a large program funnel.
ACEA reported global car production of 78.7 million units in 2025 and registrations of 77.6 million units.
Higher vehicle output keeps OEM sourcing active across floor systems and dash pads.
Interior damping parts are specified by platform.
Each new model cycle can reset supplier positions through material cost and acoustic proof.
The market is divided into five primary segment groups based on system type and material type.
Vehicle class, application area and sales channel complete the structure.
System type covers floor systems and dash panels.
Door pads and headliners are included.
Trunk liners complete the group.
Material type covers foam fiber composites and polyester felt.
Rubber mats and natural fiber are included.
Recycled PET completes the material scope.
Vehicle class covers passenger cars and SUVs.
Light trucks and commercial vehicles are included.
Electric vehicles complete the vehicle class scope.
Application area covers floor and dash.
Doors and roof are included.
Trunk area and wheelhouse liners are included.
Cockpit panels complete the application scope.
Sales channel covers OEM line fit and tier 1 modules.
Retrofit kits and MRO replacement complete the sales channel scope.
Electric vehicles reduce engine noise inside the cabin.
Road noise and panel vibration become more noticeable during city and highway driving.
IEA expects electric car sales to reach 23.0 million in 2026 with 28.0% share of total car sales.
Vehicle makers need acoustic packages that can address new sound sources.
Suppliers with test data for motor whine and tire noise can enter platform work earlier.
Acoustic panels must meet price targets set during vehicle program planning.
Recycled PET and engineered composites can cost more than basic felt in some regions.
Cost pressure is stronger in entry-level vehicles as comfort content has strict limits.
Suppliers need local material access and low-scrap molding routes.
Cost control can decide whether a material enters high-volume platforms.
Automakers are asking for lighter trim and clearer material traceability files.
Autoneum says its Ultra-Silent frunk uses up to 70.0% recycled PET and is designed for full recyclability.
Such systems prove how acoustic function and recycled content can work in one part.
Low-scrap manufacturing can improve supplier margins.
Recycled panels support OEM sustainability targets.
Recycled technical textiles give suppliers a clearer route for cabin acoustic material recovery.
Competitive positioning depends on proven cabin acoustic performance before tooling release.
Autoneum has depth in acoustic and thermal management for light vehicles.
Adler Pelzer Group and Sumitomo Riko serve insulation and sound control needs across vehicle platforms.
FORVIA’s agreement to sell its Interiors Business Group to Apollo Funds in April 2026 indicates a reorganization of interior assets.
Demand for automotive interior plastic components supports suppliers able to combine surface trim with hidden acoustic layers.
Autoneum strengthened China access by completing a 70.0% majority stake acquisition in Jiangsu Huanyu Group in February 2025.
Antolin received public funding in March 2026 for electric vehicle interior component work.
Sumitomo Electric began a tender offer in October 2025 to make Sumitomo Riko a wholly owned subsidiary.
Supplier restructuring points toward closer control over acoustic materials and interior module capability.
China is forecast to lead country demand at 7.2% CAGR through 2036.
The market is estimated at USD 1,100.0 million in 2026 and is forecast to reach USD 2,204.7 million by 2036.
This creates USD 1,104.7 million in incremental opportunity for China.
China matters because EV production and local vehicle programs run at large scale.
Cabin noise control has become more important as electric powertrains make tire sound and floor vibration easier to hear.
India is forecast to expand at 6.8% CAGR during the forecast period.
The market is estimated at USD 350.0 million in 2026 and is forecast to reach USD 675.7 million by 2036.
This creates USD 325.7 million in incremental opportunity for India.
India demand comes from passenger vehicle programs and SUV output.
Automakers need cost-controlled acoustic packages that improve cabin comfort without adding too much weight.
India is unique because SUV demand and cost-sensitive vehicle programs move together.
Suppliers need acoustic parts that are light and affordable for local assembly plants.

The market is estimated at USD 300.0 million in 2026 and is forecast to reach USD 547.5 million by 2036.
This creates USD 247.5 million in incremental opportunity for Mexico.

Acoustic panels made in Mexico often need to match cabin comfort standards for vehicles sold in the United States.
Mexico is unique because export assembly and acoustic part conversion sit inside the same regional supply corridor.
Suppliers that serve North American platforms can use Mexico for lower freight cost and faster line support.

Cabin quietness carries value because Korean vehicles compete in markets that compare comfort and interior quality closely.
South Korea is unique because OEMs use domestic suppliers for export-ready interiors.
Acoustic insulation must meet repeat quality needs across EVs and SUVs built for global sales.

EV output increases the need for lighter floor systems and recycled acoustic panels with verified performance.
Germany is unique because acoustic performance is a clear part of premium vehicle quality.
Suppliers must prove part weight, sound reduction and material recovery before program approval.
Foam fiber composites lead material demand in Germany.
Recycled PET is expected to move faster as OEMs request lower-weight cabin materials.

Key sub-regions covered in the United States include Michigan, Ohio, Tennessee, Kentucky, Alabama, Texas, and South Carolina.

facts: SK Innovation is implementing new immigration compliance measures at its $2.6 billion battery plant project in Jackson County, Georgia.
Growing evidence suggests foreign workers are displacing American workers during the plant's construction.
SK Innovation instituted daily document checks on Thursday to verify worker authorization.
U.S. Representative Doug Collins requested an investigation by ICE into the company's hiring practices a week prior.
In an August 19 letter, Rep. Collins cited concerns about Korean workers being imported to build the plant amid rising COVID-19 job losses in Georgia.
Rep. Collins sent a second letter on Thursday to the heads of ICE and U.S. Customs Border Protection (CBP).
The congressman reported that over 30 single-family homes were being rented to house Korean laborers, potentially violating local occupancy rules.
Local officials and constituents informed Rep. Collins that approximately 200 workers, seemingly all Korean, were receiving welding training at a defunct chicken farm five miles from the plant site.
Rep. Collins cited rumors that American building inspectors found job site work insufficient and failed welds on mechanical systems due to improper procedures.
Rep. Collins urged the U.S. government to suspend visas for SK's direct employees until contractor hiring practices are corrected.
In two May incidents at Hartsfield-Jackson Atlanta International Airport, customs officials blocked 33 arriving Korean workers destined for the plant.
Customs officials also rescinded Electronic System for Travel Authorization (ESTA) clearance for an additional 25 Korean workers.
CBP accused the detained workers of using fraudulent employment letters and belonging to a criminal network trying to take American jobs.
Due to a lack of daily flights to Seoul, detained workers were sent to a Lovejoy detention center before deportation, with the first 12 held for multiple days.
SK Innovation maintains that site worker hiring is the responsibility of its contractors.
SK Innovation stated that over 1,000 American workers have been employed at the construction site to date.
SK Innovation established a security checkpoint on Thursday barring anyone without clear U.S. work authorization from entering the site.
SK Battery America (SKBA) stated it will impose severe sanctions, including potential contractor termination, for policy violations.
SKBA will conduct ongoing inspections to ensure contractor compliance with applicable laws.
Local residents and union leaders expressed grievances over the Korean workforce presence while local tradespeople faced unemployment or underemployment.
A Fox 5 news crew documented Korean workers traveling to rented homes, implying violations of immigration laws.
Subcontractors featured in the Fox 5 video include Korea-based firms KapJin and Younchang, which supply clean-room, power-generation, and HVAC systems to SK Innovation globally.
SK Innovation told the Korea Times it is importing workers with experience from high-tech battery plants, such as its facility in Hungary.
The company cited a tight construction timeline and existing battery supply agreements with Volkswagen and Ford for the U.S. market.
Some contractor workers may qualify for legal exceptions to U.S. foreign labor prohibitions.
The Visa Waiver Program allows citizens of 38 countries (including Korea) to visit the U.S. for up to 90 days without a visa.
Under the program, business activities like prospect meetings and supervision are permitted, but skilled/unskilled labor is forbidden.
Exceptions allow foreign commercial workers to install, repair, or maintain equipment purchased overseas if included in the sales contract.
The program arguably permits foreign workers to enter the U.S. to train Americans on operating or maintaining such machinery.
SK Innovation declined to identify its contractors or provide further comment on the situation.
CBP declined to disclose which companies provided fraudulent employment letters or name the denied workers.
Immigration attorney Teri Simmons noted that Southeast manufacturing facilities often purchase specialized machinery (CNC machines, conveyors, robotics) from foreign suppliers.
Simmons stated CBP in Atlanta is typically fair-minded and business-minded regarding immigration law application.
Simmons confirmed that technicians frequently travel legally under the Visa Waiver Program to install equipment.
CBP reported that the detained Korean workers intended to be paid $6,000 to $7,000 for two to three months of work at the Commerce, Georgia plant site.
This pay rate raised concerns among officials that foreign labor would undercut U.S. wages.
German automakers previously faced similar criticism when contractors brought workers via ESTA and B1 visas to build plants in Alabama and South Carolina.
Contractors for Tesla and Volvo have also been accused of similar visa misuse, as documented by CBS News.
Simmons recommended that all inbound foreign companies thoroughly study U.S. immigration requirements to avoid compliance issues.
Workers seeking Visa Waiver exceptions must provide documentation like overseas purchase contracts and clearly explain their supervisory or installer roles.
Pandemic-related travel bans shut down consulates and embassies, hindering business travelers from applying for national interest exceptions.
President Trump issued a June proclamation barring non-immigrant visas (L-1 and J-1) through the end of 2020.
Korean Deputy Consul General Kwangsuk Lee reported that Consul General Young-jun Kim is urging Korean companies to prioritize U.S. immigration compliance for sustainable investment prospects.
SK Battery's project is part of a wave of Korean investment in Georgia since Kia Motors established operations there in 2007.
The project received an incentives package worth $300.3 million, adding sensitivity to the immigration controversy.
Jackson County Area Chamber of Commerce Director John Scott clarified that the incentives only apply to Phase 1 ($1.67 billion investment, 2,000 jobs), not Phase 2 ($940 million, 600 jobs).
Incentives do not apply during construction; $53 million in state job tax credits will be distributed only after 2,000 permanent American jobs are created over five and a half years.
Most other tax credits are available to all Georgia companies based on spending in areas like research and development.
The largest incentive component involved land costs, including a $28.2 million purchase with interest and $102 million in tax relief over two decades, effectively making the land free.
Jackson County retains title to the land and all installed equipment for 20 years.
Approximately $160 million is expected to be generated for local schools from the site, which previously generated almost no tax revenue.
John Scott noted that most of the $300 million in incentives would not exist if SK had chosen another state.
If SK leaves or shuts down early, it must repay the entire cost of the land.
John Scott reported no knowledge of the welding operation cited by Rep. Collins and noted constant communication with SK, which denies inappropriate worker entry.
Scott characterized the issue as an isolated incident and highlighted positive spillover effects for local neighborhoods and restaurants.
SK has already spent millions with local contractors in Jackson County and across the U.S., spending heavily with U.S. citizens regardless of ancestry.
Scott explained that bringing over Korean workers is necessary due to the highly technical nature of lithium-ion battery equipment and potential trade secrets like machine settings.
SK Innovation faces patent violation accusations from rival LG Chem, with an initial International Trade Commission (ITC) determination issued in February.
A Seoul court ruled in favor of LG Chem in a civil suit challenging LG's U.S. actions, with the ITC scheduled to issue a final ruling in October.
SK Innovation maintains that plant construction remains on schedule despite legal disputes.
The Georgia Department of Economic Development (GDEcD) is actively recruiting Korean investment but expects full compliance with employment laws without exception.
GDEcD noted that similar rumors occurred during Kia's plant construction a decade ago, yet Kia has since built highly-rated vehicles and created tens of thousands of jobs in West Georgia.
SK Battery has donated over $500,000 to Georgia educational programs and coronavirus response initiatives.
Electrolyte recycler Enchem plans to build a $63 million plant near the SK facility.
Additional SK suppliers and unrelated Korean prospects are considering Jackson County following awareness of Georgia's investment climate.
Governor Brian Kemp traveled to Korea last summer to meet with SK and other potential investors.
Georgia shifted economic development resources from China outreach to target Korean firms during an opportune recruitment window.
Korean mattress manufacturer Zinus announced on August 18 plans for a $108 million factory and distribution center in McDonough, creating 804 jobs.
source_title: SK Battery Installs Immigration Checks Amid Criticism of Hiring Practices at Korean-Owned Plant - Global Atlanta

facts: Honda is investing over $1 billion to retool the Marysville Auto Plant, East Liberty Auto Plant and Anna Engine Plant to create the flexibility to build gas-powered, hybrid, and battery-electric models on the same production lines.

The Marysville Auto Plant in Ohio produces the Accord, Acura Integra, Accord hybrid, and Type S variant, with an annual capacity of 220,000 vehicles, employment of 4,400 workers, and a total investment of $5.5 billion.
The Marysville plant performs subassembly of the hybrid battery unit for the Accord hybrid and is the exclusive global production facility for the Acura Integra.

The East Liberty Auto Plant in Ohio produces the CR-V, CR-V hybrid, Acura MDX, and MDX Type S, with an annual capacity of 240,000 vehicles, employment of 2,600 workers, and a total investment of $1.9 billion.

The East Liberty plant is part of the company’s retooling efforts to support flexible manufacturing of gas-powered, hybrid and battery-electric vehicles on the same production line in the Honda Ohio plants.

The Performance Manufacturing Center in Marysville, Ohio, began production of the Honda Fastport eQuad micromobility delivery vehicle in April 2016 and the CR-V e:FCEV hydrogen fuel cell electric vehicle in 2024.

The Anna Engine Plant in Ohio produces engines for multiple Honda hybrid models, including the 2.0-liter Atkinson-cycle four-cylinder engine, and began production of a 3.0-liter turbo V6 engine for Acura Type S models in 2021 as part of a $200 million plant expansion.

The Alabama Auto Plant in Lincoln, Alabama, produces the Pilot, Passport, Odyssey, Ridgeline, TrailSport models, and their V6 engines, with an annual capacity of 340,000 vehicles, employment of 4,500 workers, and a total investment of $3 billion.
The Indiana Auto Plant in Greensburg, Indiana, began production of the Civic Hatchback hybrid following a $50.2 million investment in September 2021 to prepare the plant for mass production.

The Georgia Transmission Plant in Tallapoosa, Georgia, produces state-of-the-art 10-speed automatic transmissions for the Honda Pilot, Passport and Odyssey and the Acura MDX, with an annual capacity of 375,000 V-6 transmissions, employment of 480 workers, and a total investment of $485 million.
The Georgia plant expanded from 100,000 to 514,000 square feet in 2017 and invested an additional $100 million for a new assembly line and other modifications.

The Ohio Transmission Plant in Russells Point produces two-motor hybrid systems that help power the Honda Accord hybrid, Civic hybrid and CR-V hybrid trims, with an annual capacity of 500,000 hybrid power systems and employment of 1,200 workers.

L-H Battery Company, Inc., a joint venture between Honda and LG Energy Solution established in 2022, is located in Jeffersonville, Ohio, and began production of pouch-type lithium-ion batteries for EVs and hybrids in 2026 with a projected investment of $4.4 billion.
The L-H Battery Company facility spans over 2 million square feet and will also produce Energy Storage Systems alongside hybrid batteries.

Honda Canada Manufacturing plants in Alliston, Ontario, announced an investment of $1.38 billion over six years in 2022 to upgrade manufacturing plants in preparation for production of electrified vehicles, starting with the CR-V hybrid.
Total U.S. vehicle production reached 32,903,583 units as of 2025, with the Accord reaching 12,674,388 units and the CR-V reaching 2,995,166 units.
Total U.S. hybrid battery production for the Accord hybrid reached 449,786 units as of 2025, while CR-V hybrid production reached 691,897 units.

Multiple Honda U.S. facilities including Marysville, East Liberty, Anna Engine, Alabama, Indiana, Georgia Transmission, Ohio Transmission, and Canada plants earned the 2025 U.S. Environmental Protection Agency ENERGY STAR Certification.
Several Honda U.S. facilities operate as zero-waste-to-landfill plants, including the Performance Manufacturing Center, Alabama Auto Plant, and Georgia and Ohio Transmission Plants.
Hyundai Motor Group plans to break ground on a new electric vehicle factory near the Georgia coast.
The proposed plant site is located in Bryan County, Georgia.
The total investment for the new EV facility is $5.5 billion.
President Joe Biden signed the Inflation Reduction Act (IRA) into law last month, complicating Hyundai's manufacturing plans.
Prior to the IRA's signing, electric vehicles from Hyundai, Kia, and Genesis qualified for federal tax credits of up to $7,500.
In May, President Biden publicly praised Hyundai executives for selecting the Bryan County site for the future plant.
Under the newly signed IRA, EVs assembled overseas no longer qualify for the $7,500 tax credit.
The Hyundai Ioniq5 and Kia EV6 are currently assembled in South Korea and thus lose eligibility for the price reduction under the new law.
To qualify for the $7,500 tax credit, pickups, SUVs, and vans must be priced at less than $80,000.
All other vehicle types must cost less than $55,000 to qualify for the tax credit.
Buyers must have an annual income of less than $150,000 to be eligible for the tax credit.
Final assembly of qualifying vehicles must take place in the United States, Canada, or Mexico.
Battery minerals and other components cannot be sourced from "foreign entities of concern," which explicitly include China and Russia.
The IRA is designed to discourage overseas production and force automakers to build domestic supply chains for the North American market.
The law aims to spur more domestic production of EVs while shifting supply chains away from foreign adversaries like China and Russia.
Hyundai's reliance on overseas assembly for models like the Ioniq5 and EV6 conflicts with the IRA's domestic assembly requirement.
The article notes that the tax credit structure is intended to compel automakers to establish domestic manufacturing and supply chain operations.
source_title: Inflation Reduction Act a wrench in Hyundai plans for Georgia electric vehicle plant

facts: The third generation Kia Optima is built and manufactured in West Point, Georgia, which began in 2011 with the 2012 model.
US specification models will be assembled at Kia's West Point, Georgia assembly plant (KMMG).
Production of the K5 in the United States ended in 2024 with the KMMG plant shifting its focus to the Telluride and Kia's EV lineup.
American models are now sourced from Korea.

In September 2011, the Optima Hybrid set a Guinness World Record for 'Lowest Fuel Consumption in a Hybrid Gasoline Vehicle' while driving across the continental United States for 14 days, starting from the Kia factory in West Point, Georgia.
The Optima Hybrid uses the Hyundai Sonata Hybrid powertrain, combining a 2.4-liter MPI engine with a six-speed automatic transmission.
The Optima Hybrid powertrain includes a 30kW electric motor and lightweight lithium polymer batteries to produce a full gasoline-electric hybrid.
The Optima Hybrid has an estimated fuel consumption of 37mpg‑US (6.4L/100km; 44mpg‑imp) city and 39mpg‑US (6.0L/100km; 47mpg‑imp) highway.
The plug-in hybrid variant features a 50kW electric motor and 2.0-liter 'Nu' four-cylinder GDI engine, mated to a six-speed automatic transmission.
Retail deliveries of the Kia Optima PHEV began in the United States in January 2017 with 40 units sold.
Georgia has no state EV purchase rebate, though a prior $5k EV tax credit expired in 2015.
In Georgia, EVs pay a $200 annual registration fee.
Georgia allows HOV/Toll lane access with an AFV tag for EVs.
Some utility rebates are available in Georgia, such as Georgia Power offering a $250 charger rebate.
Atlanta offers parking perks for EVs.
Volvo is building a U.S. factory in Ridgeville, South Carolina in partnership with Polestar.
Volvo has plans for American-built EVs, including the upcoming EX90 electric SUV.
Volvo currently operates a manufacturing plant in South Carolina that produces the S60 sedan.
Volvo's South Carolina plant will produce the upcoming EX90 electric vehicle.
