facts: Aurubis commenced production at its Richmond, Georgia facility in late 2023.
The Richmond facility is Aurubis' first greenfield plant constructed in 115 years and marks its first major manufacturing presence in the United States.
The Georgia recycling facility has an annual output capacity of 70,000 metric tons of high-grade blister copper.
The facility uses advanced recycling technology to process complex materials and copper-containing scrap into valuable intermediate products.
Aurubis is exploring three expansion pathways for its US operations: a new copper smelter, an expansion of the existing Georgia facility, or a second recycling plant on the West Coast.
Expanding the Georgia facility would involve adding an anode furnace and tank house to produce finished copper cathodes instead of intermediate blister copper.
The proposed Georgia expansion could include rod production capabilities to directly serve wire and cable manufacturers.
The Georgia facility expansion offers a reduced implementation timeline of approximately 3-4 years compared to building a new smelter.

Aurubis CEO Toralf Haag stated that preliminary discussions with U.S. government officials regarding support for a new domestic smelter have yielded "positive signs," though concrete proposals are still in development.
The United States currently has only three operating copper smelters, including Aurubis' Georgia plant, compared to approximately 60 in China and 15 in Europe.
Current U.S. refined copper demand is approximately 1.7 million metric tons annually, with domestic production covering only about 50% of the requirement.
U.S. copper demand is projected to surge to 2.3 million metric tons by 2035, representing a 35% increase from current levels.
Recent U.S. tariffs on copper products exempt raw materials like ores, concentrates, and cathodes to incentivize domestic processing and retain scrap within domestic markets.
Copper is essential for electric vehicle production, charging networks, renewable energy infrastructure (solar, wind, transmission), AI data centers, defense manufacturing, and telecommunications.
The U.S. copper recycling market is projected to expand by 26% over the next decade, increasing annual recycling volumes to approximately 555,000 metric tons.
Aurubis set its European customer premium at a record $315 per metric ton for 2024, marking a 38% increase from the previous year.
Benchmark copper prices on the London Metal Exchange recently reached a 16-month peak of $11,000 per ton.
Aurubis is simultaneously planning a 500 million euro investment in its Hamburg facilities for a new precious metal refinery and complex recycling plant.
Expansion of copper processing capacity would generate high-skilled industrial jobs including production operators, maintenance technicians, laboratory specialists, engineers, and management roles.
Recycled copper requires up to 85% less energy to process than copper produced from ore, resulting in substantially lower carbon emissions per ton of output.
Aurubis' advanced processing technology enables the recovery of precious metals (gold, silver, platinum, palladium) and industrial metals (tin, nickel, antimony) from complex scrap materials.
Modern copper recycling facilities incorporate advanced air filtration systems, closed-water circuits, and waste heat recovery systems to minimize environmental impact.
Electric vehicles use 3-4 times more copper than traditional vehicles, driving sustained demand growth in the EV sector.

Government support mechanisms for copper industry development can include tax incentives, grants or low-interest loans, expedited permitting, infrastructure development, R&D partnerships, tariff protection, and energy cost subsidies.
source_title: Aurubis Plans US Copper Smelter with Government Support

facts: Average residential electricity rate in Georgia is 15.540 ¢/kWh.
Program data was last updated on November 21, 2025.
There are 51 nationwide federal incentives that typically apply in all states.
Georgia has created 2 state-level EV incentive programs.
There is 1 utility rebate or program offer from electric utilities serving Georgia.

The U.S. Department of Transportation Federal Aviation Administration (FAA) Zero Emissions Airport Vehicle and Infrastructure Pilot Program provides funding to airports for up to 50% of the cost to acquire ZEVs and install or modify supporting infrastructure.
FAA pilot program grant funding must be used for airport-owned, on-road vehicles used exclusively for airport purposes.
Vehicles and infrastructure under the FAA pilot program must meet the Federal Aviation Administration's Airport Improvement Program requirements, including Buy American requirements.
The FAA pilot program gives priority to applicants located in nonattainment areas defined by the Clean Air Act and projects that achieve the greatest air quality benefits per dollar of funds spent.

Georgia offers an Alternative Fuel Vehicle (AFV) Conversion Tax Credit providing an income tax credit for 10% of the cost to convert a vehicle to natural gas, electricity, propane, and hydrogen, up to $2,500 per vehicle.

Converted vehicles under the AFV tax credit must be fueled solely by an alternative fuel and meet emissions standards for low-emission or zero emission vehicles defined by the Board of Natural Resources.
Unused portions of the Georgia AFV tax credit may be carried forward for up to five years, but beginning January 1, 2025, the carryforward period is reduced to up to three years.
Any motor vehicle equipped with idle reduction technology in Georgia may exceed state gross, axle, and tandem weight limits by up to 550 pounds.
To qualify for the idle reduction weight exemption, operators must present written certification of the technology's weight and demonstrate or certify that it is fully functional at all times.
Residential customers of Georgia Power may receive up to a $200 rebate for purchasing and installing a 208/240-volt Level 2 charger in a single-family home or townhome.
The Georgia Power residential charger rebate requires a dedicated circuit for the charger, application within 6 months of installation, and excludes third-party vendors.

The U.S. Department of Energy provides grants of up to $200,000 for research and development of commercial innovations related to EV chargers, EV batteries, and biodiesel, hydrogen, and fuel cell vehicle technologies.
Domestic small businesses are eligible applicants for the DOE AFV R&D grants.

The U.S. Department of Transportation's Federal Transit Administration offers grants through the Buses and Bus Facilities Program to replace, rehabilitate, and purchase buses, vans, and related equipment, and construct associated bus facilities including low or zero emission vehicles or facilities.
FTA Buses and Bus Facilities Program funding may be requested for workforce development training or training at the National Transit Institute.

Eligible applicants for the FTA program include state, local, and tribal governments that allocate funds to or operate fixed-route bus services, with private nonprofit organizations engaged in public transportation eligible as subrecipients.

The U.S. Department of Transportation established a carbon reduction formula program for states to reduce transportation emissions through activities like truck stop electrification, diesel engine retrofits, vehicle-to-infrastructure communications equipment, public transportation, port electrification, and deployment of alternative fuel vehicles including charging or fueling infrastructure.
At the request of a state, DOT must provide technical assistance in developing a carbon reduction strategy that must be updated at least once every four years.
State projects under the DOT carbon reduction formula program are treated as Federal-aid Highway Program projects.

The U.S. Department of Transportation's Federal Highway Administration Charging and Fueling Infrastructure Discretionary Grant Program offers funding to deploy publicly accessible EV charging and alternative fueling infrastructure in urban and rural communities and along Alternative Fuel Corridors.
The CFI Program offers two funding types: Community Charging and Fueling Grants and Alternative Fuel Corridor Grants, with varying award amounts and cost share requirements.
Community Program infrastructure deployments must be located on public roads or publicly accessible locations and prioritize low-income, underserved, rural, and high-density communities.
Corridor Program grants are available for infrastructure deployments along designated AFCs and for educational and community engagement activities.
Eligible applicants for the CFI Program include metropolitan planning organizations, U.S. territories, special purpose districts and public authorities, and state, local, and tribal governments.

Clean Construction and Clean Agriculture are voluntary programs promoting reduction of diesel exhaust emissions from construction and agricultural equipment by encouraging proper operations, maintenance, use of emissions-reducing technologies, and cleaner fuels.

Clean Construction and Clean Agriculture are part of the U.S. Environmental Protection Agency's Diesel Emissions Reduction Act Program, which offers funding for clean diesel construction and agricultural equipment projects.

The U.S. EPA Clean School Bus program provides funding to replace existing school buses with clean, alternative fuel, or zero emission school buses, potentially awarding up to 100% of the cost for the bus, charging equipment, or fueling infrastructure.
Eligible alternative fuels under the EPA Clean School Bus program include electricity, natural gas, hydrogen, and propane.

Eligible applicants for the EPA Clean School Bus program include school districts, state and local government programs, federally recognized Indian tribes, nonprofit organizations, and eligible contractors.

The EPA prioritizes funding for high-need local education agencies, low income, rural and tribal schools, and applications that cost share through public-private partnerships, grants, or school bonds.
Beginning January 1, 2023, a federal tax credit is available to businesses and tax-exempt organizations for purchasing new EVs and FCEVs.
New EVs/FCEVs with a GVWR below 14,000 lbs must have a battery capacity of at least 7 kWh, while those above 14,000 lbs must have at least 15 kWh.
The federal Commercial EV and FCEV Tax Credit amount equals the lesser of 15% of purchase price for PHEVs, 30% for EVs/FCEVs, or the incremental cost compared to an equivalent ICE vehicle.
Maximum federal tax credits are capped at $7,500 for vehicles under 14,000 lbs and $40,000 for vehicles above 14,000 lbs.
Businesses cannot combine the Commercial EV/FCEV Tax Credit with the Clean Vehicle Tax Credit.

The U.S. DOE's National Renewable Energy Laboratory supports communities through the Energy to Communities Program by offering in-depth partnerships, peer-learning cohorts, and one-on-one matches with experts.

Eligible communities for the NREL E2C Program include local governments, tribal governments, metropolitan and regional planning authorities, utilities, community-based organizations, transit agencies, school districts, housing authorities, and universities.
The U.S. DOT and DOE Joint Office of Energy and Transportation offers grants of up to $4 million for projects expanding community e-mobility access and providing clean reliable energy.

Joint Office grant topics include solving no-home charging, electrifying light- and medium-duty fleets, and developing managed charging programs, with a minimum 50% cost share requirement for demonstration and deployment projects.
Eligible applicants for Joint Office grants include universities, businesses, nonprofit organizations, and state, local, and tribal governments.

The Congestion Mitigation and Air Quality Improvement Program provides funding to state DOTs, local governments, and transit agencies to meet Clean Air Act requirements by reducing mobile source emissions and regional congestion.

CMAQ eligible activities include transit improvements, travel demand management, congestion relief, diesel retrofit projects, alternative fuel vehicles and infrastructure, and medium- or heavy-duty ZEVs with related charging equipment.
CMAQ projects must demonstrate emissions reductions, be located in or benefit an EPA-designated nonattainment or maintenance area, and qualify as transportation projects.

The U.S. DOE provides grants for transportation decarbonization research with priority given to cost-effective EV charging deployment for those without home access, innovative mobility solutions for underserved communities, and R&D to reduce EV battery size, cost, range limitations, and emissions.

DOE EV charger grant priorities also include electrification of off-road and non-road vehicles, materials technologies to improve EV efficiency and affordability, use of alternative fuels in commercial off-road technologies, and planning for medium- and heavy-duty EV charging and hydrogen fueling corridors.
Applicants for DOE EV charger grants must demonstrate how proposed projects will benefit underserved communities lacking access to clean transportation options.

The U.S. DOT FHWA EV Charger Reliability and Accessibility Accelerator Program offers funding for repairing and replacing existing non-operational publicly accessible Level 2 and DC fast chargers, covering up to 80% of eligible project costs.
Eligible applicants for the FHWA EV Charger Accelerator include state departments of transportation and local governments.

The U.S. Department of Housing and Urban Development offers funding for utility connections supporting EV charging infrastructure through the HOME Investment Partnerships Program, available to state and local governments as grants, direct loans, loan guarantees, credit enhancements, rental assistance, or security deposits.

The U.S. DOE offers free EV deployment technical assistance to local governments through the Energy Ready Charging Smart Program, which provides roadmaps for EV deployment and helps lower infrastructure deployment costs.

The U.S. USDA Rural Energy for America Program provides loan guarantees and grants to agricultural producers and rural small businesses for renewable energy systems or energy efficiency improvements, including EV charging stations.

Eligible EV charging stations under the USDA REAP program may only be used for private fleet purposes and cannot dispense electricity for retail use, with a maximum grant award of 25% of project costs subject to congressional appropriations.

The U.S. DOE Office of Manufacturing and Energy Supply Chains established the ITAC Program to provide clean energy resources for small- to medium-sized manufacturers, universities, career institutions, and individuals seeking hiring and training opportunities.

DOE offers grants of up to $300,000 per project for ITACs to develop EV conversion playbooks for small- and medium-sized manufacturers, with eligible applicants including universities, community colleges, trade schools, union training programs, and public sector-supported applicants.

The U.S. DOE Communities Local Energy Action Program Pilot facilitates sustained economic and environmental benefits through clean energy deployment work, offering technical assistance to up to 36 low-income, energy-burdened communities experiencing direct environmental justice or economic impacts from shifting away from fossil fuels.

The U.S. DOT FHWA Federal Lands Access Program provides funding for transportation facility improvements on or adjacent to Federal lands, with eligible electric mobility projects including light-duty EV charging, public transportation charging, commercial charging infrastructure planning, workforce development, and vehicle acquisition.
Eligible applicants for the FHWA FLAP program include state DOTs, local governments, and tribal governments, and projects must meet the FHWA EV Charging Minimum Standards Rule.

The U.S. DOT INFRA grant program provides federal financial assistance to eligible transportation infrastructure projects addressing climate change and environmental justice impacts, including supporting modal shifts to reduce vehicle miles traveled, developing ZEV infrastructure, using demand management strategies, and installing EV chargers along the National Highways System.

Eligible applicants for DOT INFRA grants include states, metropolitan planning organizations serving urbanized areas with populations over 200,000, local governments, political subdivisions, port authorities, and tribal governments.

The U.S. EPA offers grants for heavy-duty ZEVs and associated infrastructure that may cover up to 100% of total project costs, including incremental replacement costs for Class 6 or 7 vehicles, capital/installation/operation/maintenance costs for charging/refueling infrastructure, workforce development training, and planning activities.

Eligible applicants for EPA heavy-duty ZEV grants include state governments, municipalities, public school districts, tribal governments, and nonprofit school transportation associations, with additional funding available for projects in nonattainment communities.

The American-Made Challenges are prize competitions partnered with the National Renewable Energy Laboratory designed to incentivize entrepreneurs to spur manufacturing, develop innovative solutions, and create domestic jobs through public-private partnerships.

The U.S. DOE offers grants of up to $2 million for large-scale renewable energy planning and siting projects, including EV chargers, through the R-STEP Program, with eligible applicants including state-based collaboratives led by state energy offices and universities.

The U.S. DOT FTA Low or No Emission Grant Program offers grants to local and state government entities for purchasing or leasing low or zero emission transit buses and supporting facilities, with funding available for workforce development training at the National Transit Institute.
Applicants for the FTA Low No Program using ZEVs must submit a long-term fleet management plan detailing how funds will be used for resources and acquisitions.

facts: Bridgestone Corporation is a Japanese multinational tire and rubber manufacturing company based in Kyōbashi, Tokyo.
The company was founded on 1 March 1931 by Shojiro Ishibashi in Kurume, Fukuoka, Japan.
Key people include Yasuhiro Morita (Global CEO).
Products include tires, golf equipment, and automotive components.
Bridgestone supplies automakers with vibration-isolating components, such as engine mounts.
Bridgestone also supplies air springs for trucks, automobiles and train carriages.
Bridgestone markets aluminium wheels and other automotive accessories.
As of July 2018, the Bridgestone Group has 181 production facilities in 24 countries.
The company opened its first postwar overseas plant in Singapore in 1965 and commenced production in Thailand in 1969.
Bridgestone Americas was established in 1967 to serve as the U.S. sales representative branch.

In 1983, the company purchased a plant in Tennessee from Firestone Tire and Rubber Company for its first North American manufacturing facility and started production of radial tires for trucks and buses.
Bridgestone operates a steel cord plant in Clarksville, Tennessee, named Bridgestone Metalpha, which is ranked as the top provider in the global steel cord market.
In April 2012, Bridgestone Americas opened a new $100 million Bridgestone Americas Technical Center in Akron that houses 450 employees.
In June 2022, Bridgestone opened a $21 million Advanced Tire Production Center in Akron that produces racing tires for the NTT IndyCar Series and all Firestone Firehawk racing tires.
Bridgestone opened a $6 million passenger tire test track adjacent to the Akron facility in Autumn 2022.
Bridgestone has invested more than $125 million in its Akron operations since 2012.

Nashville-based Bridgestone Americas, Inc. develops, manufactures and markets Bridgestone, Firestone, and associate brand tires for consumers, automotive and commercial vehicle original equipment manufacturers, and those in the agricultural, forestry and mining industries.
The company also produces air springs, roofing materials, synthetic rubber and industrial fibers and textiles.
In 2017, Bridgestone Americas consolidated many business units into a single building in downtown Nashville where nearly 2,000 employees work.

In April 2020, Bridgestone Americas announced plans to restart its North American commercial tire plants and Firestone Industrial Products and Firestone Building Products manufacturing facilities due to demand during the COVID-19 pandemic.

Bridgestone Aircraft Tire moved its Miami, Florida aviation operation to a new 160,000-square-foot facility in Mayodan, North Carolina, expected to be completed by mid-2007, creating approximately 95 new jobs.

Diversified operations encompass automotive components, industrial products, polyurethane foam products, construction materials, parts and materials for electronic equipment, bicycles and sporting goods.
Bridgestone's diversified business generates about one-fourth of total sales in the Bridgestone Group.
Bridgestone was the sole tire supplier for Formula One from 1997 to 2010.
Firestone has been the single tire provider for the IndyCar Series and its feeder series Indy NXT since 2000.
From April 2023, Bridgestone became the main tire supplier for the Super Taikyu championship series.
Bridgestone was the exclusive tire supplier for MotoGP from 2009 to 2015.
Bridgestone was the Official Tire of the Olympic Games until the end of 2024.

In January 2026, Bridgestone agreed to sell its tyre reinforcement business in China and Thailand to Bekaert for €60 million as part of a strategy to enhance competitiveness, including a long-term tyre cord supply agreement.
Bridgestone EU distributes more than 25 million tires a year through 17 national sales subsidiaries and 2 distributors, directly employing over 12,000 people with Mr. Tsuda as CEO.
The plant at Ulyanovsk, Russia and the marketing office in Moscow were sold in December 2023 as Bridgestone withdrew from the Russian market following the Russian invasion of Ukraine.
Total employees numbered 129,262 in 2023.
Revenue was ¥4.11 trillion (US$37.45 billion) for the fiscal year ended 31 December 2023.
Founder Shojiro Ishibashi died on 11 September 1976.
SK Innovation is investing $1.67 billion in a new lithium-ion battery plant in Jackson County, Ga.
The batteries produced at the SK Innovation facility are used in hybrid electric vehicles.
The SK Innovation project will create 2,000 jobs.
Japan-based Toyo Tire is investing $138 million in its tire plant in Bartow County, Ga.
Toyo Tire will add 150 jobs to increase the total workforce at the Georgia plant to 2,100.
The Toyo Tire expansion adds capacity to produce over 13 million passenger car tires per year.
Mazda Toyota Manufacturing USA broke ground on November 16, 2018, for a $1.6 billion automotive assembly plant in Huntsville, Ala., and Limestone County.
The Mazda Toyota Manufacturing plant will house 4,000 employees across two production lines.
One line at the Mazda Toyota facility will assemble the Toyota Corolla model.
The second line at the Mazda Toyota facility will build a new Mazda crossover SUV.
BMW has started production of the X7 Sports Activity Vehicle at its Spartanburg County, S.C., plant.
The BMW X7 is assembled on the same line as the X5 and X6 models.
BMW originally announced a $1 billion investment for its Spartanburg County plant in 2014.
BMW constructed a new 1.2 million-square-foot body shop at its South Carolina facility that houses over 2,000 new robots.
The BMW Spartanburg plant produces approximately 1,400 vehicles per day.
Seventy percent of the vehicles produced at the BMW South Carolina plant are exported to over 140 global markets.
BMW is considering building a second engine plant in North America to support its largest worldwide facility in Spartanburg County, S.C.
BMW currently imports its engines from Europe to its U.S. plant and has been discussing a new domestic engine plant for several years.
Volvo is reducing hiring at its $1.1 billion assembly plant in Berkeley County, S.C., due to President Trump's trade war with China.
Volvo selected South Carolina in 2015 for its first U.S. plant, which opened for full production of the S60 sedan in the summer.
Volvo had planned to hire 1,500 employees by the end of the year and up to 4,000 when starting a second line for an SUV.
Volvo global CEO Hakan Samuelsson announced the hiring reduction at the South Carolina plant.
Hyundai CEO Wonhee Lee stated the automaker may soon make a significant investment in its Montgomery, Ala., plant.
Hyundai is considering adding two additional production lines to its Alabama facility: one for an SUV and one for a new pickup truck.
The Hyundai Alabama plant currently assembles two sedans and one SUV.
New Flyer of America cut the ribbon on a $25 million renovation and expansion of its Anniston, Ala., factory.
The New Flyer expansion is the culmination of $50 million in facility improvements over the past two years.
The expanded New Flyer plant now employs 750 workers.
New Flyer manufactures battery-electric transit buses at its Alabama facility.
India-based auto supplier Sundaram-Clayton Limited is expanding its campus in Ridgeville, S.C., near Charleston.
Sundaram-Clayton manufactures aluminum casting products for Mercedes-Benz Vans and is investing $40 million while hiring 100 workers.
Swedish auto supplier Gnotec Group is investing $5.9 million to open its first U.S. plant in Orangeburg County, S.C.
The Gnotec facility will create 78 jobs specializing in the engineering and manufacturing of metal components for automotive and commercial vehicles’ body-in-white and chassis structures.
Automotive supplier Truform Manufacturing is investing approximately $14 million to expand its operations in Dickson, Tenn.
Truform Manufacturing plans to create nearly 90 jobs over the next five years at its Tennessee facility.
Auto supplier Hwashin America Corp is expanding its Greenville, Ala., manufacturing operation with a $26 million investment in new equipment and 50 new jobs.
Hwashin America produces body components for Hyundai’s Alabama auto assembly plant and has been in expansion mode since arriving in Butler County in 2003.
Nissan’s Smyrna factory was approved for $158 million in upgrades to continue hiring for a workforce of 8,400 and improve the plant.
The automaker obtained authorization from the Rutherford County Industrial Development Board in November for two bonds totaling $158.1 million for upgrades including new machinery and equipment.
The Tennessee Valley Authority contracted with First Solar and NextEra Energy Resources to construct the largest solar installations ever built in Tennessee and Alabama.
The TVA solar farms will power Facebook’s new $750 million data center in Huntsville, Ala., with 100 percent renewable energy.
First Solar will erect a 227-megawatt solar farm in Colbert County, Ala.
NextEra Energy Resources will build a 150-megawatt solar farm in Lincoln County, Tenn.
Facebook required all of its electricity to be generated from renewable sources as a condition for locating its data center in Alabama.
Florida-based NextEra Energy Resources is looking to invest $75 million in a large solar farm project in Houston County, Ala.
The Houston County Commission approved a 10-year property tax abatement for 1,000 acres for the NextEra Energy solar project.
If completed as planned, the NextEra Energy Alabama solar farm will be finished in 2021.
Exports of South Carolina-made BMWs continue to drop due to tariffs, prompting plans to move more SUV production to China.
BMW already manufactures the X3 at its China plant and might move production of the X5 model there as well.
In October, BMW exported 13,702 vehicles from its South Carolina plant through the Port of Charleston, marking a 16 percent drop from the previous year and the fourth consecutive monthly decline.
BMW officials publicly stated that the U.S.-China trade war will cost the company $344 million this year.
The Goodyear Tire & Rubber Company is an Ohio corporation organized in 1898 with principal offices at 200 Innovation Way, Akron, Ohio 44316-0001.
In 2023, the company reported net sales of $20,066 million and a Goodyear net loss of $689 million.
The company develops, manufactures, distributes, and sells tires for most applications and also manufactures and sells rubber-related chemicals for various applications.
Goodyear operates approximately 950 retail outlets where it offers its products for sale to consumer and commercial customers and provides repair and other services.
The company manufactures its products in 55 manufacturing facilities across 22 countries, including the United States, with marketing operations in almost every country worldwide.
Goodyear employs approximately 71,000 full-time and temporary associates worldwide.
On June 7, 2021, Goodyear completed its acquisition of Cooper Tire & Rubber Company for cash and stock consideration totaling approximately $3.1 billion.
On November 15, 2023, the company announced a transformation plan called "Goodyear Forward" intended to optimize its portfolio, deliver significant margin expansion, and reduce leverage.

Goodyear Forward aims to deliver gross proceeds in excess of $2 billion from portfolio optimization by pursuing strategic alternatives for its chemical business, the Dunlop brand, and its off-the-road tire business.
The transformation plan targets cost reduction actions driving an annual run-rate benefit of $1 billion by the end of 2025.
The plan also targets top-line actions driving an annual run-rate benefit of $300 million by the end of 2025.
Goodyear aims to double its segment operating income margin to 10% by the end of 2025 as part of the transformation plan.
The company operates through three regional tire business segments: Americas, Europe, Middle East and Africa (EMEA), and Asia Pacific.
Approximately 86% of Goodyear's sales in 2023 were for tire units, while sales of chemical products to unaffiliated customers accounted for 2% of consolidated sales.
Worldwide tire unit sales totaled 173.3 million in 2023, comprising 87.3 million in the Americas, 49.9 million in EMEA, and 36.1 million in Asia Pacific.
In 2023, Goodyear sold 130.2 million replacement tire units and 43.1 million original equipment (OE) tire units worldwide.
Bridgestone and Michelin are Goodyear's two major worldwide competitors in the tire industry.
Other significant tire competitors include Continental, Hankook, Kumho, Nexen, Pirelli, Sumitomo, Toyo, Yokohama, and various regional manufacturers.
The Americas segment manufactures tires in eight plants in the United States, two plants in Canada, and six plants in Brazil, Chile, Colombia, Mexico, and Peru.
Goodyear's Americas commercial business launched new tires under the Goodyear RangeMax line, providing a regional drive tire for emerging commercial electric vehicles.
The EMEA segment manufactures tires in fifteen plants located in France, Germany, Luxembourg, Poland, Serbia, Slovenia, South Africa, and Turkey.
The Asia Pacific segment manufactures tires in nine plants located in China, India, Indonesia, Japan, Malaysia, and Thailand.
Synthetic and natural rubber are the principal raw materials used by Goodyear, with synthetic rubber accounting for approximately 50% of all rubber consumed in 2023.
Goodyear's plants located in Beaumont and Houston, Texas supply a major portion of its global synthetic rubber requirements.
As of December 31, 2023, approximately 39,700 of Goodyear's associates were covered under collective bargaining agreements.
Approximately 5,300 US associates are covered by a master collective bargaining agreement with the United Steelworkers (USW) that expires in July 2026.
Approximately 2,200 associates at Goodyear's Texarkana and Findlay plants in the US are covered by separate USW agreements expiring in June 2024.

Goodyear is experiencing increased labor-related costs and manufacturing inefficiencies associated with a tight labor supply in the U.S., prompting accelerated hiring and adjusted investment plans to account for qualified worker availability.
The company owns approximately 1,700 product, process, and equipment patents issued by the USPTO and approximately 4,400 patents issued or granted in other countries worldwide.

entity_name: City of East Point Economic Development Department
facts: East Point’s Economic Development Department serves as a resource for business recruitment, retention and expansion, assisting with site selection, potential incentives (including job tax credits), and attractive financing products.
The city is located Inside the Perimeter (ITP) nestled between major interstates, with close proximity to Hartsfield-Jackson Atlanta International Airport and downtown Atlanta.
East Point has a Qualified Federal Opportunity Zone under the Tax Cuts and Jobs Act, allowing investors to defer federal taxes by reinvesting capital gains in designated low-income communities.
As a Tier 3 County, East Point qualifies businesses to apply for Tier 3 level job tax credits offering $1750 per eligible job with a minimum of 15 jobs created.
Several areas in the city are identified as HUB Zones, providing federal preferential consideration for set-aside contracts and a 10% price evaluation preference in open contracts.

The Department of Economic Development partnered with Public Works to be awarded a nearly $5 million LCI grant for transportation, pedestrian, and cycle-related improvements, with additional phases approved and underway in planning and construction.
The city operates its own Power Company through MEAG/Electric Cities of Georgia, offering extremely competitive utility rates.
East Point maintains its own private Water Reservoir (Sweet Water Creek), providing abundant water for industrial, commercial, and residential needs independent of Atlanta’s supply.

The East Point Corridors Redevelopment Plan and Tax Allocation Bond District was approved in 2006 to stimulate investment in the commercial core and historic neighborhoods along Main Street, Cleveland Avenue, and Washington Road.
The Camp Creek / I-285 TAD was approved in 2001 to support redevelopment efforts.
The Camp Creek Business Center contains 2.9 million square feet of industrial and office space with capacity for an additional 2.0 million square feet.
The Camp Creek Business Center has created more than 1,500 jobs and is positioned near a labor pool of more than 1.2 million people aged 20 to 64 within a 30-minute radius.
Duke Realty provides acreage to support up to 2.0 million square feet of new industrial or office space at the Camp Creek Business Center.
The Camp Creek Shopping District features 1.2 million square feet of retail space, with Camp Creek Marketplace at 98% occupancy and lease rates over $25 per square foot.
Lawrence Avenue Industrial Park encompasses over 200 acres and is adjacent to Norfolk Southern Railroad’s industry yard with daily switching service.
William C. Meredith Company has operated a manufacturing plant in Lawrence Avenue Industrial Park since 1921, producing Pentachlorophenol treated poles for electric utilities and municipalities.

Oakleigh Industrial Park hosts PPG’s architectural coatings plant, which began East Point operations in 1952 and manufactures latex and oil-based paints for residential, commercial, protective/marine, automotive refinish, and industrial markets.
Southmeadow Business Park includes tenants such as Porsche, the Georgia Department of Revenue, Medical Depot, and Martin Brower.
McDonald Development Company owns 150 acres at Southmeadow Business Park available for development to accommodate individual users up to 1 million square feet.
Willingham Industrial Park supports automotive repair shops, warehousing facilities, manufacturing, and recycling operations along Central Avenue with access to I-85.
The city recently adopted its first Small Business Incentives Program offering permit and license fee waivers plus tax and utility discounts for businesses with 9 employees or less.

The East Point Business and Industrial Development Authority (EPBIDA) issues tax-exempt and taxable industrial development bonds to provide state and local ad valorem tax advantages through structured sale/leaseback transactions.
EPBIDA board members are appointed by seated City Council members for four-year terms, with the board meeting on the first Thursday of each month at 6:30 pm.
The city is updating its Economic Development Strategic Plan (EDSP) to align strategies over the next 10 years with resident and business community needs.
A Transit Oriented Development (TOD) Plan completed in fall 2012 outlines catalyst redevelopment sites near East Point and Lakewood/Ft. McPherson MARTA stations along US 29.

canonical_name: Dr. Ing. h.c. F. Porsche AG
facts: Dr. Ing. h.c. F. Porsche AG is a German automobile manufacturer specializing in luxury, high-performance sports cars, SUVs and sedans, headquartered in Stuttgart, Baden-Württemberg, Germany.
The company is owned by Volkswagen AG, a controlling stake of which is owned by Porsche Automobil Holding SE.
Porsche's current lineup includes the 911, Panamera, Macan, Cayenne, and Taycan.

In May 2011, Porsche Cars North America announced plans to spend $80–$100 million to move its North American headquarters from Sandy Springs, a suburb of Atlanta, to Aerotropolis Atlanta on the site of the old Ford Hapeville plant adjacent to Hartsfield–Jackson Atlanta International Airport.
The headquarters relocation will receive about $15 million in economic incentives.
Designed by architectural firm HOK, the new North American headquarters facility will include a new office building and test track at One Porsche Drive.
In October 2017, Porsche Cars North America announced the launch of Porsche Passport, a sports car and SUV subscription program initially available in Atlanta.
During the COVID-19 pandemic in March 2020, Porsche suspended its manufacturing in Europe for two weeks.

In August 2022, Bloomberg News reported that Porsche lined up interest in its initial public offering for a valuation between US$60–85 billion, expected to be listed on the Frankfurt Stock Exchange in September.
The company has a long-standing relationship with Volkswagen, as Ferdinand Porsche designed the first Volkswagen Beetle.
In 2009, Porsche entered an agreement with Volkswagen to merge car manufacturing operations by 2011 to form an 'Integrated Automotive Group'.
As of the end of 2015, Porsche SE holds a 52.2% control interest in Volkswagen AG.
In February 2022, Volkswagen AG announced it would examine the feasibility of a possible IPO of Porsche AG, with share capital divided into 50% non-voting preference shares and 50% ordinary shares.
Volkswagen AG will retain 75% of ordinary shares while Porsche SE acquires 25%, and Qatar Investment Authority committed to buy 4.99% of preference shares.
As part of the preliminary offering, 113,875 thousand shares were sold at 82.5 euros, estimating the company's value at 75 billion euros.
In April 2022, Porsche Australia announced plans to open an e-fuel manufacturing facility in Tasmania named the HIF Tasmania Carbon Neutral eFuel Plant, slated to open in 2026.
Porsche’s operating profit in 2025 collapsed by roughly 98%, falling from approximately €5.3 billion in 2024 to €90 million.
The company reversed a high-value investment in electric vehicle research and production, resulting in a heavy write-down of €3.9 billion restructuring charge due to BEV sales stagnation.
United States tariffs increased to 15% and briefly 25% on importing 'finished vehicles', impacting Porsche's operations.
Global sales dropped 10% from 310,718 in 2024 to 279,449 in 2025.
New CEO Michael Leiters is focusing on a leaner, more efficient model lineup while reducing reliance on volume, maintaining a full year 2026 forecast of a 5% to 7% profit margin range.
Porsche's headquarters and main factory are located in Zuffenhausen, Stuttgart, where it produces flat-6 and V8 piston engines.
Cayenne and Panamera models are manufactured in Leipzig, Germany, with SUV parts also assembled at the Volkswagen Touareg factory in Bratislava, Slovakia.
Since 2011, the Zuffenhausen plant area has more than doubled from 284,000m2 to 614,000m2 following purchases of former Layher, Deltona and Daimler sites.
In May 2025, Porsche North America confirmed that production for all current 718 Boxster and 718 Cayman variants is scheduled to end in October 2025.
Porsche CEO Oliver Blume confirmed future production of full-electric replacements for the 718 lineup will arrive in the 'medium term.'

facts: Tredegar Corporation is an industrial manufacturer based in Richmond, Virginia, operating through Aluminum Extrusions and Electronic Materials segments.
Bonnell Aluminum is Tredegar's largest segment and a leading North American manufacturer of custom-finished aluminum extrusions.
Key markets for Bonnell Aluminum include the automotive sector, specifically for structural components and battery housings.
The company offers comprehensive services including design, extrusion, painting, anodizing, and fabrication for its aluminum products.
Tredegar maintains long-term relationships with top-tier automotive OEMs who rely on its precise tolerance levels and finishing quality.
The push for Electric Vehicles (EVs) is a massive catalyst for aluminum extrusions, as aluminum is preferred for EV frames and battery trays due to its strength-to-weight ratio.
Tredegar’s 2024-2025 strategy focuses on capital expenditures for Bonnell Aluminum expansion, particularly in the automotive sector.
The "Near-shoring" trend in North American manufacturing prioritizes domestic supply chains for automotive parts over overseas imports, serving as a long-term tailwind for Tredegar.
New trade tariff policies, such as the Section 232 tariff increase on imported aluminum, protect Tredegar's domestic market share.
In 2025 Q4, the aluminum extrusion segment sales grew 26.2% year-over-year, driven by robust demand in U.S. automotive markets.
The company successfully passes rising metal costs to downstream customers to maintain profit margins in the automotive and construction sectors.
Analysts note that Tredegar's strategic refocusing aligns with growing demand for electric vehicle components.
Bonnell Aluminum remains one of the top 5 independent (non-integrated) extruders in North America.
According to Grand View Research, the North American aluminum extrusion market is projected to grow at a CAGR of ~4.5% through 2030.
Tredegar reported net income of $1.0 million for full year 2024, rebounding from a $99.2 million loss in 2023.
Ongoing operations earnings reached $17.2 million ($0.50/share) as of Q3 2024.
Following the 2024 divestiture of its Terphane business, Tredegar reduced its net leverage ratio from 3.7x to 1.1x by March 2025.
The company sold Terphane for approximately $116 million (subject to closing adjustments) to pivot toward specialized industrial niches.
As of 2025 year-end, Tredegar had approximately $87 million in available credit facility (ABL Facility) for potential acquisitions or capital expenditures.
Major institutional holders include BlackRock Inc., The Vanguard Group, and Dimensional Fund Advisors.

facts: Global aluminum extrusion market size calculated at USD 99.61 billion in 2025 and expected to hit around USD 224.18 billion by 2035 from USD 108.03 billion in 2026, growing at a CAGR of 8.45% from 2025 to 2035.
Electric vehicle manufacturers rely heavily on extruded aluminum for battery housings, structural reinforcements, and lightweight body parts that improve efficiency and performance.
Between 2025 and 2034, the aluminum extrusion industry is expected to experience accelerated growth, particularly in high-margin sectors such as construction, electronics and automotive.
Increasing adoption of electric vehicles is expanding demand for lightweight structural components, battery housings and thermal management solutions made from extruded aluminum.
Shift toward sustainable building materials in residential and commercial projects across the Asia Pacific, Europe and North America is strengthening demand.
Global expansion is driven by strategic acquisitions, capacity increases and new production facilities across North America, Europe and Asia Pacific.

Norsk Hydro ASA recently opened a new aluminum recycling plant in Michigan, United States, to support the North American market with high-recycled-content extrusions and improved supply-chain resilience.

Major investors including Hindalco Industries Limited, Alcoa Corporation, Norsk Hydro ASA, and Constellium N.V. are investing in modern extrusion presses, upgraded die technologies, automation, and high-speed finishing lines to improve precision and output.

Investors are focusing on technologies that enhance scrap utilization, increase recycled content and support the production of specialty extrusions for electric vehicles, renewable energy systems and aerospace components.
Market size value in 2026 is USD 108.03 billion with a revenue forecast of USD 224.18 billion in 2035, reflecting a CAGR of 8.45% from 2026 to 2035.
Fastest growing region is North America, driven by growing development of fuel-efficient vehicles and rise in adoption of electric vehicles.

Automakers in North America are increasingly using lightweight aluminum components to improve performance, reduce emissions, and extend driving range, strengthening need for extruded parts in frames, battery housings, and structural components.
United States is a key contributor to the aluminum extrusion sector due to growing expansion of the transportation sector and rise in electric vehicles.

Growing production of building materials such as structural elements, window frames, and curtain walls requires aluminum extrusion, with construction companies relying on it for energy-efficient building systems.

Europe is growing rapidly in the market due to strong focus on reducing vehicle emissions and rise in electric vehicles, with automakers replacing heavier steel components with lightweight aluminum parts to meet emissions targets and improve energy efficiency.
Demand for extruded profiles used in engine systems, battery structures, and body frames is strengthened by the European EV trend.
Germany is growing rapidly due to growing production of vehicles and rise of electric mobility, with German automakers focusing on lightweight engineering and energy efficiency.
Extruded aluminum components are increasingly used in battery housings, safety structures, and interior support systems in Germany.

Innovations such as automated processes and die design supported by advanced machinery, improved tooling, and digital process control help German manufacturers enhance product quality, reduce waste, and increase production efficiency.
South America market set to grow from USD 5.19 billion in 2025 to USD 11.84 billion by 2035 with expected CAGR of 8.57% over forecast period from 2026 to 2035.

Automakers in South America are increasingly adopting extruded aluminum parts to reduce vehicle weight and improve performance, while residential construction uses extruded profiles for windows, doors, railings, and façade elements.

Brazil contributes to market growth due to manufacturing of fuel-efficient vehicles and focus on lowering vehicle emissions, with automakers using lightweight aluminum parts to improve performance and meet emission reduction goals.
Extruded aluminum is commonly used in chassis components, structural supports, and thermal management systems in Brazil, aligning with efforts to modernize its vehicle fleet.
Renewable energy installations depend on extruded aluminum for mounting structures, support beams, electrical housings, and frame systems due to durability and ease of fabrication.

Key companies profiled include Alupco, Arconic Corp., Bahrain Aluminum Extrusion Company, Constellium N.V., Gulf Extrusions Co. LLC, Hindalco Industries Ltd., Kaiser Aluminum, Norsk Hydro ASA, and QALEX.

Feedstock procurement involves sourcing raw materials such as aluminum logs or aluminum billets, with key players including Hindalco Industries Limited, Rio Tinto Aluminum, Norsk Hydro ASA, and Alcoa Corporation.

Chemical synthesis and processing involve bauxite refining, smelting, lubrication, quenching, heat treatment, and surface finishing, with key players including Kemira Oyj, Aditya Birla Chemicals, Grauer and Weil Limited, BASF SE, and Huber Engineered Materials.

Quality testing evaluates tensile strength, hardness, dimensions, and functional defects, with certifications including BIS, ISO 14001, ISO 9001, and IATF 16949; key players include Element Materials Technology, Intertek, WINTECH, and SGS.

Step-by-step manufacturing process includes Die & Billet Preparation, Heating (approx 400-500 °C), Extrusion via hydraulic press, Cooling, Straightening & Cutting, Aging, and Post-Extrusion Fabrication.
Shapes segment dominated market accounting for 64.40% in 2025 due to growing development of automotive components such as engine blocks, battery housings, chassis, and anti-intrusion beams.
Pipes and tubes segment is fastest-growing with CAGR of 6.4% during forecast period due to strong focus on lowering vehicle weight and rise in electric vehicles requiring pipes & tubes.

7000 series segment growing at 6.3% CAGR due to automotive frames, high-performance aircraft components, chassis development, focus on lowering vehicle weight, and rise in electric vehicles requiring the 7000 series.

Automotive segment is fastest-growing in market with 6.2% CAGR due to stricter CO2 emissions regulations, focus on reducing vehicle weight, rise in electric vehicles, and growing manufacturing of body structures, crash management systems, interior parts, and chassis.
Hot extrusion segment dominated market accounting for 70.3% in 2025 due to increased manufacturing of vehicle components and structural construction elements.
Cold extrusion segment expected to grow fastest with 6.2% CAGR due to growing production of precision parts such as heat sinks, engine components, and chassis components.

facts: REX Shares and Tuttle Capital Management announced the launch of the T-REX 2X Long TE Daily Target ETF (Cboe: TEUP), a leveraged ETF providing 2x daily long exposure to T1 Energy Inc. (NYSE: TE).
T1 Energy is an energy solutions provider developing an integrated U.S. supply chain for solar and batteries.
As one of the leading solar manufacturing companies in the U.S., the company operates G1_Dallas, a 5GW solar module manufacturing facility.
T1 Energy is set to open a second manufacturing site, G2_Austin, in late 2026.

Scott Acheychek, COO of REX, stated that TEUP gives traders exposure to T1 Energy at the center of the U.S.’s clean energy buildout, combining solar manufacturing scale with a battery storage strategy.
Matt Tuttle, CEO and CIO of Tuttle Capital Management, described T1 Energy as advancing its mission to build a homegrown U.S. solar and storage supply chain.
The launch expands the T-REX ETF suite, which now includes over 40 leveraged and inverse single-stock ETFs.
T1 Energy shares dropped more than 4.4% in premarket trading after short seller Fuzzy Panda Research announced it had taken a short position in the company.
Fuzzy Panda published a report alleging that T1 Energy is not compliant with Foreign Entity of Concern (FEOC) regulations and may not qualify for certain U.S. tax credits.

The report claims T1’s transfer of intellectual property to Singapore-based Evervolt was intended to establish FEOC compliance, but Evervolt maintains undisclosed ties to Chinese solar manufacturer Trina Solar.
Fuzzy Panda stated that Evervolt is owned by Tan Chin Piaw, who the firm alleges has maintained business relationships with Trina Solar for more than 15 years.
Patent records reportedly indicate the intellectual property remains under the ownership of Trina Solar rather than Evervolt.
According to the short seller, 99% of T1 Energy’s first-quarter revenue was generated through Trina Solar.
Fuzzy Panda questioned the company’s eligibility for U.S. tax credits, alleging that T1 recorded $41.4 million in tax credits during the first quarter of 2026 despite not yet receiving them.

The report noted Internal Revenue Service guidance issued in February 2026 established a July 4, 2025 deadline for intellectual property licensing agreements tied to FEOC compliance, while T1’s agreement with Evervolt was reportedly dated December 29, 2025.
The report additionally alleged that construction of T1’s G2 solar cell manufacturing facility is behind schedule.
Fuzzy Panda cited drone footage taken in early May that it claims shows limited construction progress and estimated the project is running 12 to 18 months behind schedule.
T1 management has previously stated that the facility remains on track to begin operations in the fourth quarter of 2026.
The report also stated that T1 Energy has received subpoenas from both the U.S. Department of Justice and the Securities and Exchange Commission.
T1 CEO Daniel Barcelo was scheduled to discuss the future of American energy dominance live at 4 PM EST on February 5, 2026.
Freyr Battery selected Austin, Texas, for its new global headquarters.

facts: The automotive electronics industry in the U.S. focuses on integrating electronic systems within vehicles, including infotainment solutions, safety features, and advanced driver-assistance systems.
Companies in this field range from giant manufacturers like HARMAN International to smaller firms focusing on niche products.
The listed companies represent a cross-section of the U.S. automotive electronics market, with headquarters primarily in Michigan and global operations.
Most companies were founded in the 20th century and have adapted to technological shifts toward electric and autonomous vehicles.
HARMAN International is an audio electronics company based in Stamford, Connecticut, founded in 1980.
HARMAN designs connected products for automotive, consumer, and enterprise markets, focusing on advanced audio products and infotainment systems.
HARMAN is a wholly-owned subsidiary of Samsung.
HARMAN employs over 27,000 people globally, with substantial presence in India, the United States, and other countries.
Yazaki North America is based in Canton, Michigan, and has been operational since 1966.
Yazaki specializes in producing wire harnesses, connectors, and power distribution systems for modern vehicles.
Yazaki serves major automotive manufacturers and is actively developing technologies for hybrid and electric vehicles.
Dura Automotive Systems LLC (Dura-Shiloh) is based in Auburn Hills, Michigan, founded in 1914.
Dura-Shiloh specializes in mobility systems including electrification solutions, lightweight structural systems, and advanced safety technologies.
Dura-Shiloh's product portfolio includes battery enclosures for electric vehicles, park-lock actuators, and shift-by-wire systems.
Wells Vehicle Electronics is based in Fond Du Lac, Wisconsin, operating since 1903.
Wells specializes in vehicle electronic components like ignition systems and engine management sensors.
Inteva Products was founded in 2008 and headquartered in Troy, Michigan.
Inteva offers Closure Systems, Interior Systems, and Motors & Electronics to over 100 global automakers.
Inteva received $755 million in funding in May 2019.
The Intec Group is a private manufacturing company based in the United States with significant presence in Mexico, founded in 1953.
Intec specializes in fuel systems, engine electronics, and door systems for Tier One automotive manufacturers.
GHSP is based in Holland, Michigan, founded in 1924, specializing in mechanical and electromechanical systems.
GHSP provides advanced driver controls and smart powertrain technologies that integrate electronic controls and motors.
Sumitomo Electric Wiring Systems, Inc. (SEWS) is based in Bowling Green, Kentucky, founded in 1986.
SEWS manufactures automotive wiring harnesses, electrified vehicle components, and advanced product development services for EVs and hybrids.
Pacific Insight Electronics Corp. is a subsidiary of Methode Electronics, based in Durham, California, founded in 1989.
Pacific Insight provides user interfaces, LED lighting, sensors, and powertrain components for automotive OEMs.
Nartron was founded in 1967 and is based in Reed City, Michigan.
Nartron specializes in advanced electronic systems including iQ Power®, Clearfast®, and Smart Close® for automotive and military sectors.
AISIN World Corp. of America is based in Northville, Michigan, serving as the North American sales headquarters for AISIN Corporation.
AISIN manufactures parts for hybrid and electric vehicles, including brakes, transmissions, and navigation systems.
Rostra Accessories was founded in 1987 and is based in Aberdeen, North Carolina.
Rostra produces electronic cruise control systems, vehicle camera systems, and parking assistance technologies.
Pi Innovo was founded in 2011 and is based in Plymouth, Michigan.
Pi Innovo specializes in embedded control units and software solutions for electric and hybrid vehicles.
AAMP Global was founded in 1987 and is based in Clearwater, Florida.
AAMP operates through its Stinger brand, offering high-quality audio systems, infotainment radios, cameras, and lighting solutions for the automotive aftermarket.
DeAmertek Corporation was founded in 1977 and is based in Lombard, Illinois.
DeAmertek specializes in Electric Power Assisted Steering (EPS), Adaptive Front Lighting Systems (AFS), and Fuel Delivery Modules (FDM).
Weastec, Inc. was founded in 1988 and is based in Hillsboro, Ohio.
Weastec produces HMI switches, sensors, and control modules for OEMs and operates a technical center in Dublin, Ohio.
Ehps (Dare Auto Inc.) was founded in 2013 and is based in Plymouth, Michigan.
Ehps specializes in brushless DC motors, electronic control systems, Electro Hydraulic Power Steering, keyless entry, and tire pressure monitoring systems.
Nidec Elesys Americas Corporation was founded in 2002 and is based in Suwanee, Georgia.
Nidec Elesys develops automotive safety systems and electronic control units, including occupant detection systems, radar technology, and electronic stability control (ESC) systems.
